(3) Can learners orally complete the sentence: 'The maize plant wilts at noon because…' — target answer should include 'transpiration rate exceeds water uptake rate by roots.' Use exit observation: before moving to DQB phase, ask all learners to hold up 1 finger if they can define transpiration, 2 fingers if they can also explain cohesion-tension theory — use this quick pulse check to decide whether to pause for clarification.

framework.learnerExperience: Each learner writes one sticky note (or a square on a piece of paper) responding to the prompt: 'What new evidence or understanding from today can I add to help answer our driving question?' Learners also write one new question that today's lesson raised for them. The class gathers at the DQB (a section of the board or a large paper posted on the wall). The teacher facilitates a 4-minute whole-class discussion to sort contributions into two columns: 'Evidence we now have' and 'Questions we still need to answer.' Key new evidence cards added today include: 'We can SEE that leaves release water vapour (plastic bag)' and 'Transpiration pull drags water up xylem — explains the upward journey.' New questions raised might include: 'What makes stomata open and close?' or 'Does the rate of transpiration change?' or 'Does transpiration happen at night?' These questions are flagged as targets for Lesson 5 (factors affecting transpiration).
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Look at our Driving Question Board from Lessons 1, 2, and 3. In Lesson 1, we asked how water enters roots. In Lesson 2, we showed it crosses the cortex by osmosis. In Lesson 3, we saw xylem vessels carry water upward. Today — what have we added?' Point to the board and WAIT 10 seconds. Ask learners to write their sticky notes/cards individually (2 minutes). Then say: 'Come up one row at a time and add your evidence card and your question card.' As learners place cards, read 3–4 of them aloud and ask: 'Does anyone want to challenge or add to this card?' Use cold-call for 3 learners. Explicitly connect the new evidence to the phenomenon: 'The Kericho farmer's maize: can we now answer WHY it wilts at noon? Thumbs up if yes, thumbs sideways if partly, thumbs down if still unsure.' Acknowledge: 'We can now explain the wilting. Our next lesson will answer: why does the rate change with weather conditions?' Circle or star the question 'What controls how fast transpiration happens?' on the DQB as the bridge to Lesson 5.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board Update (Evidence Mapping): The DQB is a living sensemaking tool that externalises the class's growing understanding. By physically placing evidence cards and question cards, learners see their own knowledge construction over the 8-lesson sequence — this is the NGSS practice of Engaging in Argument from Evidence applied at the class level. The visual accumulation of evidence on the board builds a sense of scientific progress and shows learners how individual lessons connect to the overarching driving question.
framework.formativeAssessment: Read all sticky notes/cards after class. Assess: (1) Are evidence statements specific (e.g., 'Transpiration pull in xylem') or vague ('plants lose water')? — specificity indicates depth of understanding; (2) Are new questions generative (pointing toward factors affecting transpiration, stomatal control) or confused (going backward to already-answered questions)? — quality of questions is a strong formative indicator of conceptual clarity; (3) Count how many learners gave a 'thumbs up' to the phenomenon explanation — target is at least 70% confident before proceeding to Lesson 5.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners retrieve their cumulative plant-transport model from their exercise books (started in Lesson 1 and updated each lesson). Working individually, they add the following to their model: (1) An arrow at the leaf labelled 'TRANSPIRATION — water vapour lost through stomata'; (2) A large upward arrow along the xylem labelled 'TRANSPIRATION PULL (cohesion-tension)'; (3) A small annotation near the stomata showing 'Stomata open → water evaporates → tension created in xylem → water pulled up.' Learners also add a 'WHY WILT?' annotation box on the diagram, writing: 'At midday in Kericho — transpiration rate > water uptake rate → xylem tension not supplied fast enough → leaves lose turgidity → wilt.' Partners exchange books for a 1-minute peer check using three criteria written on the board: Does the model show WHERE transpiration happens? Does it show HOW it creates pull? Does it connect to the Kericho maize wilting?
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Open your cumulative models. Look at what you drew in Lessons 1, 2, and 3. Today you are going to make your model more complete — add the DRIVING FORCE that you now understand.' Write the three addition prompts on the board (transpiration arrow, pull arrow, stomata annotation) and give learners 5 minutes to update their models. Circulate and look for: models that show the pull arrow going DOWNWARD (a common error — address immediately: 'The tension is created at the TOP and pulls water UP — which way should your arrow point?'). After individual work, direct peer exchange: 'Swap with your partner. Use the three criteria on the board to give one specific piece of feedback.' After 1 minute, cold-call 2 learners to share what feedback they gave. Close with: 'Your model now shows the complete journey of water from soil to the atmosphere. The Kericho farmer's maize plant is doing this right now, today. Tomorrow we investigate what makes that arrow faster or slower.' Preview Lesson 5 by writing on the board: 'Factors that affect the rate of transpiration.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision (Progressive Model Building): The running model is the central sensemaking artefact of the 8-lesson sequence, embodying the NGSS practice of Developing and Using Models. Each lesson's additions make the model more mechanistically complete. The peer-review using explicit criteria introduces scientific critique — learners evaluate each other's models against explanatory standards, not just neatness. The 'WHY WILT?' annotation box is a deliberate technique to ensure learners do not treat the model as an abstract diagram but as an explanation tool anchored to the real-world phenomenon.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher examines 6–8 models during circulation (targeting learners who showed uncertainty on the 'thumbs' check). Specific indicators to assess: (1) Transpiration arrow is at the LEAF, not the root — tests understanding of where water exits; (2) Pull arrow is UPWARD along xylem — tests directionality of cohesion-tension force; (3) The 'WHY WILT?' box uses causal language ('because,' 'therefore,' 'when…then') — tests ability to construct a causal explanation. Collect 4 books after class for detailed written feedback. Note: any learner whose model still shows water being 'pushed up' rather than 'pulled up' by transpiration requires a brief one-to-one correction at the start of Lesson 5.

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
1. During the Observe Phase, did most learners correctly attribute the condensation inside the plastic bag to the leaves (not the stem or the air inside the bag)? If learners were uncertain, what additional evidence or questioning could make the source of the water vapour clearer in future lessons?
2. When learners sequenced the 6 mechanism cards for cohesion-tension theory, which step caused the most confusion — was it the osmosis step (step 3–4), the tension/negative pressure concept (step 5), or the root-to-leaf directionality? How will you address this misconception in the opening minutes of Lesson 5?
3. Were learners able to connect transpiration pull to the Kericho farmer's midday wilting phenomenon using causal language ('because,' 'therefore')? Or did they describe the phenomenon and the mechanism as separate facts? What questioning moves could strengthen causal reasoning?
4. How many learners remained at 'thumbs sideways or down' after the DQB phase, suggesting they could not yet explain the wilting using today's evidence? What is your plan for these learners before Lesson 5 begins?
5. Did the 40-minute timeframe allow sufficient time for meaningful model revision, or was the Model Building Phase rushed? If rushed, which phase could be shortened in the next iteration — and would that compromise evidence-gathering or sensemaking quality?

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners directly observed water vapour condensing on the inside of a plastic bag sealed around a leafy branch — direct physical evidence that leaves release water to the atmosphere. They also observed from a leaf cross-section diagram that water vapour exits through open stomata after evaporating from mesophyll cell walls.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Transpiration is the loss of water vapour from aerial parts of a plant, primarily through stomata. When water evaporates from mesophyll cells, those cells lose water potential, pulling water by osmosis from xylem vessels — this creates tension (negative pressure) in the xylem column that is transmitted all the way down to the roots, pulling water continuously upward. This is the cohesion-tension (transpiration pull) theory.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The Kericho farmer's maize plants wilt at midday because hot, sunny, windy conditions drive rapid transpiration from the leaves — water vapour is lost through stomata faster than the roots can absorb and supply water from the soil. Even though the soil is moist, the demand at the top exceeds the supply from the bottom, so the xylem tension is not met, cells lose turgidity, and leaves droop. By evening, cooler temperatures and lower light cause stomata to close, transpiration slows dramatically, root absorption catches up, turgidity is restored, and the plant stands upright again — without any additional watering.

LESSONS.title: Factors Affecting Transpiration — Potometer Practical Investigation
LESSONS.aresKeywords: transpiration rate, potometer, stomata, water loss, environmental factors

slo.purpose: Learners use a locally constructed potometer to measure and compare transpiration rates under different environmental conditions, building direct evidence for why the Kericho farmer's maize plants wilt at midday despite moist soil.
slo.knowledge:
• Identify and explain how light intensity, temperature, wind, and humidity each affect the rate of transpiration in plants
• Describe how a potometer works as a tool for measuring transpiration rate
• Explain the relationship between high transpiration rates and afternoon wilting in maize plants
slo.skills:
• Construct and use a simple potometer from locally available materials to collect quantitative data on water uptake as a proxy for transpiration
• Record, tabulate, and analyse experimental data to identify patterns in transpiration rates across varying environmental conditions
slo.attitudes:
• Appreciate the importance of controlled scientific investigation in explaining everyday agricultural observations such as midday wilting in Kericho
• Demonstrate patience and precision in handling delicate laboratory apparatus and recording accurate measurements
slo.keyInquiry: How does water get from the soil all the way to the top leaves of a tall maize plant — and why does the plant sometimes lose water faster than it can take it up?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This lesson provides the critical missing piece explaining the Kericho farmer's midday wilting: learners gather direct experimental evidence that high light intensity, high temperature, wind, and low humidity dramatically increase transpiration rates, so that even in moist soil, water loss from leaves can outpace uptake by roots — causing the wilting the farmer observes at midday and recovery by evening.
slo.safetyNotes: Handle razor blades or scissors used to cut plant stems with care — teacher must supervise all cutting. Ensure cut stems remain submerged in water at all times during setup to prevent air locks. If lamps are used to simulate high light intensity, keep them away from water and do not touch bulbs directly. Wipe any water spills immediately to prevent slipping.

LESSONS.overview:
In lessons 1–4, learners explored how water enters roots by osmosis, travels up xylem vessels by cohesion-tension, and exits through stomata in a process called transpiration. They know that stomata open during the day to allow photosynthesis and that water vapour escapes in the process. However, the central puzzle of the Kericho phenomenon — why does the rate of water loss sometimes exceed the rate of water uptake? — has not yet been fully answered. This lesson directly addresses that question by placing the investigation in the hands of the learners.

Learners work in small groups to construct a simple potometer using rubber tubing, a capillary tube, and a freshly cut leafy shoot (ideally from a maize plant, bean, or locally available shrub). They systematically vary one environmental condition at a time — light (sunny vs. shaded), temperature (ambient vs. near a warm lamp or in the sun), wind (still vs. fan), and humidity (dry air vs. misting a plastic bag around leaves) — and measure the distance the air bubble moves along the capillary tube over a fixed time period as a proxy for the rate of water uptake (and therefore transpiration). Data are recorded in tables and analysed to identify which conditions produce the fastest rates.

By the end of the lesson, learners connect their quantitative data back to the driving question: the midday conditions in Kericho (bright sunshine, high temperature, dry wind) are precisely the combination that maximises transpiration rate, overwhelming the plant's ability to replace water through root uptake. By evening, cooler temperatures, lower light, and calmer air reduce transpiration, allowing root uptake to catch up and turgor to be restored. This evidence advances the class's collective model of water movement and sets up Lesson 6, which will examine the stomatal control mechanisms that regulate this balance.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown a simple diagram of the potometer setup on the board and asked to predict: 'Under which condition will the air bubble move fastest — bright sunlight, strong wind, high temperature, or high humidity?' Each learner writes their ranked prediction (1st to 4th) in their notebooks with a one-sentence justification for their top choice. Learners then share predictions with a partner using a Think-Pair-Share before the teacher takes a quick class poll by show of hands for each condition.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher draws or displays a labelled potometer diagram and asks: 'Look at this setup. We are going to test four conditions one at a time. Before we touch any equipment, I want you to rank these four conditions — bright light, strong wind, high temperature, high humidity — from the one you think will make the bubble move fastest to slowest. Write your ranking in your notebook and give one reason for your top choice. You have 2 minutes.' [Allow 2 minutes of independent writing.] After writing: 'Share your ranking and your reason with the person next to you. Does your partner agree? If not, why not? One minute.' [Allow 1 minute.] Then cold-call 4 learners — one who ranked each condition as fastest — asking each: 'You said [condition] would be fastest. Tell us your reasoning.' [WAIT TIME: 12 seconds after each cold call before accepting a response or prompting.] Teacher records the class's predicted ranking on the board without evaluating correctness: 'We are not deciding who is right yet — we are going to let the data tell us.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Prediction-before-investigation (anchored prediction): Learners activate prior knowledge about stomata and evaporation to make testable predictions. By committing predictions to paper before observing, learners create cognitive dissonance if data contradicts their prediction, which deepens engagement with the evidence. Recording predictions also allows learners to track how their thinking changes — a core NGSS Science and Engineering Practice (Planning and Carrying Out Investigations).
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans notebooks during Think-Pair-Share to check whether learners are connecting their predictions to stomatal function or surface evaporation rather than making random guesses. Listen for whether justifications reference water vapour, stomata, or evaporation rate. Red flag: learners who simply copy their partner's ranking without engagement — prompt these learners: 'Tell me in your own words why you think wind would speed it up.'

framework.learnerExperience: Working in groups of 4–5, learners assemble a simple potometer: a freshly cut leafy shoot (bean, maize, or local shrub) is inserted into rubber tubing connected to a capillary tube partially filled with water, with one end submerged in a beaker of water. Learners introduce an air bubble into the capillary tube and mark its starting position with a wax pencil. Each group tests two to three conditions sequentially (the class collectively covers all four conditions by dividing the work), measuring how far the air bubble moves in exactly 5 minutes. Learners record their raw data in a results table in their notebooks, then groups share results to produce a class data table on the board.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher circulates during setup and says: 'Before you introduce the bubble, make sure there are no air gaps in the rubber tubing — your stem must be fully submerged when you cut it, just like we discussed in Lesson 3 with xylem continuity.' [Check each group's setup — the most common error is an air lock at the tubing junction.] Once setups are confirmed, teacher says: 'Mark your bubble position with the wax pencil NOW. Start your timer. You have exactly 5 minutes. While you wait, one person should be observing the bubble, one recording in the table, and one preparing the next condition.' [Teacher circulates and cold-calls 2 learners at random: 'What does it mean if the bubble moves quickly?' WAIT TIME: 10 seconds.] After 5 minutes: 'Record the final bubble position. Calculate the distance moved. Now change your condition — if you were testing normal light, now test bright light or fan.' After all groups finish: 'Send one representative to write your group's results on the class data table on the board. We are going to read this table together.' Teacher ensures all four conditions are represented in the class data. If using a fan for wind, teacher demonstrates safe placement. If using a lamp for temperature, teacher places it at safe distance and says: 'Do not touch the bulb. Do not let the lamp contact water.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Data collection and collaborative class data synthesis (NGSS: Analysing and Interpreting Data): Individual groups collect partial data on one or two conditions, then pool results to construct a complete class dataset. This mirrors real scientific practice where datasets are shared across research teams, and it ensures every learner engages with all four conditions even in a short lesson. The physical act of watching the bubble move under different conditions creates a memorable, embodied experience that links environmental conditions to a measurable rate.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks that each group's results table has: condition tested, initial bubble position (cm), final bubble position (cm), distance moved (cm), and time elapsed (5 min). Watch for groups where the bubble does not move — this usually indicates an air lock and teacher should help them recut the stem underwater. Formative check question during the wait period: ask one learner per group 'What are you controlling — keeping the same — in this experiment?' to assess understanding of fair testing. Expected answer: time interval, size of shoot, volume of water.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners analyse the class data table to identify patterns: which condition produced the fastest bubble movement? They construct a bar graph in their notebooks using the class data (condition on x-axis, distance moved in 5 minutes on y-axis). Learners then write a conclusion linking each condition to transpiration rate, using their knowledge of stomata, water vapour concentration gradient, and evaporation. The teacher leads a guided whole-class discussion connecting the experimental results to the Kericho farmer's observation, asking learners to explain the midday wilting and evening recovery using the data as evidence.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher directs: 'Look at the class data table on the board. In your notebook, draw a bar graph — condition on the x-axis, distance moved on the y-axis. Give it a title and label your axes. You have 4 minutes.' [Allow 4 minutes.] After graphing: 'Now write two sentences: one describing the pattern you see, and one explaining WHY that condition produced the fastest rate — link it to what you know about stomata and water vapour.' [Allow 2 minutes.] Teacher then asks the class: 'According to your data, which single condition had the greatest effect on transpiration rate?' Cold-call 3 learners for their answers [WAIT TIME: 12 seconds each]. Then ask: 'Think back to the Kericho farmer. It is midday. What are the conditions around her maize plants?' Cold-call 2 learners [WAIT TIME: 10 seconds]. Expected responses: hot, bright sun, possibly windy, dry air. Teacher says: 'So if all four conditions are present together at midday, what does your data predict should happen to the transpiration rate?' [WAIT TIME: 15 seconds — this is a synthesis question; allow thinking time.] Cold-call 1 learner. Then: 'And by evening, which of those conditions have changed?' Lead learners to articulate that lower light, cooler temperature, and calmer air reduce transpiration so root uptake can catch up. Teacher explicitly says: 'This is why the plant recovers without any extra watering — the rate of loss drops below the rate of uptake, and turgor is restored.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Data-to-phenomenon connection (evidence-based explanation building, NGSS: Constructing Explanations): Learners are required to move explicitly from data (bubble distance) → mechanism (stomatal opening, water vapour gradient) → phenomenon (midday wilting, evening recovery). The bar graph makes the pattern visible and memorable. The teacher's guided questioning scaffolds the connection between laboratory data and the farmer's field observation, preventing learners from treating the practical as isolated from the central storyline.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect or photograph 2–3 notebooks to check bar graphs for correct labelling and conclusions for causal reasoning (not just correlation). Listen for whether learners use the phrase 'water vapour concentration gradient' or equivalent reasoning — if not, probe: 'You said wind speeds it up. But why? What does wind do to the air around the leaf?' Target understanding: wind removes humid air from around the stomata, steepening the gradient so water vapour diffuses out faster.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to the class Driving Question Board and identify which sticky notes from previous lessons can now be answered with today's evidence. Each group writes one new 'We now know...' statement on a green sticky note summarising what the potometer data revealed. Learners also write one new question that today's lesson has raised — for example, 'How does the plant control how wide its stomata open?' or 'Is there a point at which the plant permanently wilts?' — on a yellow sticky note and add it to the DQB.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher points to the DQB and says: 'We have had questions on this board since Lesson 1 about why the maize wilts and how water moves up. Look at the board — which questions can we now partially or fully answer using today's evidence?' Give learners 1 minute to silently review the board. Then: 'With your group, write ONE green sticky note that starts with the words — We now know that — and completes the sentence using evidence from today's potometer data. You have 2 minutes.' [Circulate and monitor — push groups who write vague statements: 'Can you be more specific? What did the data actually show?'] After groups post green notes, teacher reads 3 aloud and asks the class: 'Do you agree? Does this connect to the farmer in Kericho? Thumbs up or thumbs down.' Then: 'Now write one new question today has created for you — something you are now curious about that we haven't explained yet. Yellow sticky note. Two minutes.' Cold-call 2 learners to read their new questions to the class before posting. Teacher explicitly connects the new questions to Lesson 6: 'These questions about stomatal control — that is exactly where we are headed next.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board revision (knowledge tracking and metacognition, NGSS: Engaging in Argument from Evidence): The DQB serves as a public, cumulative record of the class's growing understanding. By distinguishing 'answered' questions from 'new questions raised,' learners practice the scientific habit of recognising that each investigation generates new questions. The colour-coded sticky note system (green = we know, yellow = new question) makes epistemic status visible and helps learners see their own progress across the 8-lesson sequence.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reads all posted green sticky notes during transition to the next phase. Adequate green notes should reference specific conditions (light, temperature, wind, humidity) and their effects on transpiration rate, not just generic statements like 'transpiration is affected by the environment.' If a group's statement is too vague, teacher returns to them: 'Good start — can you tell me which condition had the biggest effect and why?' New yellow questions should show forward-thinking curiosity about mechanisms (stomatal control, guard cells, permanent wilting point) rather than repeating already-answered questions.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners open their cumulative plant water-movement model (started in Lesson 1 and revised in each subsequent lesson) and add a new annotated layer specifically representing the factors that control transpiration rate. They add arrows, labels, and a small environmental-condition key (sun symbol for light, thermometer for temperature, wind arrow for wind, water droplet for humidity) to their model diagram, showing how each condition affects the rate of water loss from leaves. Learners also add a 'rate comparison arrow' showing that when transpiration rate exceeds uptake rate, wilting occurs, and when uptake rate equals or exceeds transpiration rate, the plant is turgid.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher says: 'Take out your cumulative model — the diagram you have been updating since Lesson 1. Today we are adding the environmental control layer. Draw four small symbols at the leaf end of your model: a sun, a thermometer, a wind arrow, and a humidity symbol. For each one, write a one-phrase note showing how it affects transpiration rate. For example: next to the sun symbol, you might write — high light = stomata wide open = fast transpiration.' [Allow 3 minutes for individual model revision.] Then: 'Now add the most important feature: draw two arrows side by side — one showing the rate of water uptake by roots, one showing the rate of transpiration from leaves. Label them. Show what happens when the transpiration arrow is bigger than the uptake arrow.' [WAIT TIME after instruction: 10 seconds to let learners interpret the task.] Cold-call 1 learner to share their model with the class — teacher holds it up or draws the key elements on the board: 'This is what [learner's name] added. Does everyone agree this captures what we learned today? What would you add or change?' [Allow 2–3 responses.] Close by saying: 'Your model now shows not just HOW water moves up, but WHY the rate of loss can exceed the rate of gain on a hot Kericho afternoon. In Lesson 6, we are going to add one more layer — the guard cells that actually regulate how wide the stomata stay open, and how the plant tries to protect itself.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative model revision (NGSS: Developing and Using Models): The running model that learners have built across all lessons acts as a personalised scientific explanation that they continually update with new evidence. Adding environmental condition symbols alongside the rate-comparison arrows forces learners to represent not just the anatomy of transport but the dynamic balance between uptake and loss — which is the core mechanism explaining the phenomenon. Model revision also reveals misconceptions: if a learner draws humidity as increasing transpiration (a common error), the teacher can address it in the moment.
framework.formativeAssessment: As learners revise models, teacher scans for three specific elements: (1) correct directionality — factors like high temperature and wind should be shown as increasing transpiration; high humidity should be shown as decreasing transpiration; (2) the rate-comparison concept — learners should show that wilting occurs when transpiration rate > uptake rate, not simply when 'no water is present'; (3) connection to stomata — the mechanism should be visible, not just correlation. Common misconception to watch for: learners who draw humidity as increasing transpiration (confusing high external humidity with more water inside the leaf). Address with: 'When the air outside the stomata is already humid, what happens to the concentration gradient for water vapour? Does water move out faster or slower?'

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
1. Did all groups successfully assemble a functional potometer and obtain measurable bubble movement? If groups struggled with air locks or bubble instability, what preparation or pre-lesson demonstration would prevent this in future?
2. Were learners able to move beyond describing the pattern in the data ('wind made the bubble move faster') to explaining the mechanism ('wind removes humid air, steepening the water vapour concentration gradient at the stomata')? Which learners need additional scaffolding on diffusion gradients?
3. Did learners make the explicit connection between the potometer data and the Kericho farmer's midday wilting phenomenon? Were there learners who treated the practical as separate from the driving question, and how can I strengthen that storyline link?
4. Looking at the DQB sticky notes, are learners generating increasingly mechanistic questions (about stomatal guard cells, wilting thresholds, hormonal control) or are they still asking descriptive questions from earlier in the sequence? What does this tell me about their readiness for Lesson 6 on stomatal regulation?
5. Did learners correctly update their cumulative models to show the rate-balance concept — that wilting is caused by transpiration rate exceeding uptake rate — rather than simply by absence of water? If not, how will I revisit this before Lesson 6 begins?

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners observed that a potometer air bubble moved at measurably different speeds under different environmental conditions: the bubble moved fastest under conditions of high light intensity, high temperature, and strong wind, while high humidity slowed bubble movement. These differences were visible within a 5-minute observation window, and the class data table showed a consistent pattern across groups.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Transpiration rate is significantly affected by environmental conditions: high light intensity causes stomata to open wider, high temperature increases the kinetic energy of water molecules and lowers humidity inside the leaf, wind removes humid air from around the stomata steepening the diffusion gradient, and high humidity reduces the concentration gradient for water vapour outward diffusion. When multiple high-transpiration conditions occur simultaneously — as at midday in Kericho — the rate of water loss from leaves can exceed the rate of water uptake by roots, causing cells to lose turgor and the plant to wilt.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The potometer data directly explains the Kericho farmer's observation: midday conditions (bright sun, high temperature, dry wind) combine to maximise the transpiration rate, so water leaves the maize leaves faster than roots can absorb it from the soil — even when the soil is moist. By evening, lower light, cooler temperatures, and calmer air reduce transpiration to a rate the roots can match, turgor is restored, and the plant stands upright again without any additional watering. This is not a failure of the soil or the roots — it is a temporary imbalance in the rate of water movement driven by environmental conditions.

LESSONS.title: Lesson 6 — Analysing Transpiration Data & Plant Adaptations to Water Loss
LESSONS.aresKeywords: transpiration rate environmental factors xerophyte adaptations potometer data

slo.purpose: Learners interpret potometer data from Lesson 5, construct and analyse graphs of transpiration rate versus environmental factors, and compare xerophyte adaptations (cactus, sisal) with mesophyte adaptations (maize, sukuma wiki) to explain how plants in different Kenyan ecosystems manage water loss.
slo.knowledge:
• Identify environmental factors (light intensity, temperature, wind, humidity) that affect transpiration rate and explain the direction of each effect
• Describe structural adaptations of xerophytes (sunken stomata, thick cuticle, water-storing tissue, reduced leaf area, CAM photosynthesis timing) and explain how each reduces water loss
• Contrast xerophyte and mesophyte leaf structures and relate differences to the water availability in their respective Kenyan habitats
slo.skills:
• Construct an accurate line graph from potometer data, correctly labelling axes, choosing appropriate scales, and plotting points with precision
• Draw evidence-based conclusions from graphical data, identifying trends and anomalies, and linking findings back to the wilting phenomenon
slo.attitudes:
• Appreciate how Kenyan farmers and agricultural scientists use knowledge of transpiration to make irrigation and crop-selection decisions
• Value the diversity of plant adaptations across Kenya's varied ecosystems, from the arid Northern Frontier to the humid Lake Victoria basin
slo.keyInquiry: How are water and mineral salts transported in plants — and what structural adaptations allow plants in dry Kenyan environments to control water loss?
slo.purposeInStoryline: Having gathered experimental evidence in Lesson 5 using a potometer, learners now make sense of that data by graphing it, identifying factor-specific trends, and connecting high transpiration rates under hot/sunny/windy conditions directly to why the Kericho farmer's maize wilts at midday; they then extend understanding to xerophyte adaptations, explaining how sisal and cactus plants in Kenya's arid regions avoid the same problem entirely.
slo.safetyNotes: No specific chemical hazards. If using cactus specimens for observation, warn learners to handle with care to avoid spine injuries. Ensure rulers and graph pencils are used safely.

LESSONS.overview:
This lesson is the analytical pivot of the evidence-gathering sequence. In Lesson 5, learners used a simple potometer (a cut shoot in a water-filled tube with a calibrated scale, assembled from locally available materials) to measure how quickly an air bubble moved under different conditions — bright sunlight versus shade, still air versus fanned air, and humid versus dry conditions. In Lesson 6, those raw data tables become the raw material for graph construction and interpretation. Learners work in small groups to plot line graphs, identify the steepest gradients, and articulate cause-and-effect explanations linking each environmental factor to stomatal behaviour and therefore to transpiration rate. This directly addresses why the Kericho farmer's maize wilts at midday: high light, high temperature, and afternoon wind all conspire to push transpiration to a rate that exceeds the root's uptake capacity.

The second half of the lesson extends the storyline beyond the maize farm. Learners examine image cards or actual specimens of sisal (Agave sisalana — a commercially important crop in Makueni and Kilifi counties) and cactus alongside maize and sukuma wiki leaves. By comparing cross-section diagrams and surface features, they identify the structural toolkit that xerophytes use to minimise the very transpiration rates that the potometer data showed were so damaging. This comparative analysis deepens the concept of structure-function relationships and introduces the idea that plants have evolved or been selected for fitness in their specific water-availability niche.

Throughout, the lesson uses data-driven argumentation — a core NGSS Science and Engineering Practice — requiring learners to cite specific data points as evidence for claims. The Driving Question Board is updated with new 'answered' and 'newly raised' questions, and the cumulative plant-water-movement model is revised to include a 'transpiration control' layer showing how environmental conditions and stomatal aperture interact.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners receive their own Lesson 5 potometer data tables (or a shared class data table on the board). Before touching graph paper, each learner writes two sentences in their science notebook predicting: (1) which environmental condition they expect will show the highest transpiration rate in their data, and (2) whether they think a sisal plant growing in Makueni would show a higher or lower transpiration rate than a maize plant in Kericho on the same day, and why. Learners share predictions with a partner (Think-Pair) before any class discussion.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the class potometer data table on the board. Say: 'Before we draw a single line on graph paper, I want you to look at this data — the numbers you collected — and predict two things. Number one: which condition gave us the fastest transpiration? Number two: if I took a sisal plant from Makueni and a maize plant from Kericho and put them side by side on a hot sunny day, which one do you predict would lose water faster — and why? Write your two predictions in your notebook now. You have 2 minutes.' WAIT: allow 2 minutes of silent individual writing. Then: 'Turn to your partner and share what you wrote. You have 90 seconds.' Cold-call 3 pairs — choose one who predicted maize would lose more, one who predicted sisal, and one who is uncertain. Record key prediction phrases on the board under the heading 'Our Predictions — We Will Test These.' Say: 'Keep these predictions visible — by the end of today you will know whether the data supports them.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Prediction Anchoring with Data: by making written predictions before graphing, learners activate prior knowledge from Lessons 3–5 (stomatal opening, transpiration pull) and create a cognitive target they are motivated to confirm or refute. Writing before sharing prevents anchoring on the first voice heard.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher circulates during the 2-minute writing window and reads 4–5 notebooks. Look for: (1) learners who can name a specific factor (e.g., 'bright light' or 'wind') rather than just 'hot'; (2) learners who reference stomata in their reasoning — these show strong carry-forward from prior lessons. Flag learners who write 'I don't know' for targeted questioning during the Observe Phase.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners work in groups of 3–4. Each group receives graph paper, a ruler, and a coloured pencil set. Using the class potometer data table, each group constructs a line graph: x-axis = environmental condition (or time in minutes if a time-series was collected), y-axis = distance moved by air bubble (mm) or rate of bubble movement (mm/min). Groups must agree on a scale before drawing. Simultaneously, each group receives an 'Adaptation Observation Card' — a printed A5 sheet showing (a) a cross-section diagram of a xerophyte leaf (labelled: thick cuticle, sunken stomata, water-storing mesophyll, reduced surface area) alongside (b) a cross-section diagram of a mesophyte leaf (labelled: thin cuticle, surface stomata, large air spaces). If actual specimens are available (a sisal leaf cutting, a cactus pad, and a sukuma wiki leaf), groups observe and touch the surfaces. Learners record 3 observable differences in a two-column comparison table in their notebooks.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute materials and say: 'Your job in the next 10 minutes has two parts. Part one: draw your line graph. Before you put pencil to paper, your group must agree on the y-axis scale — look at your highest and lowest values and choose a scale that fills at least two-thirds of the paper. Part two: study the Adaptation Observation Card and, if you have specimens, feel the surfaces. Fill in your comparison table.' Circulate actively. At the 4-minute mark, check graph scales — prompt any group with a compressed scale: 'Your scale means all your points are crowded at the bottom. What would happen if you made each small square equal to 1 mm instead of 5 mm?' At the 7-minute mark, prompt the adaptation observation by asking: 'Look at where the stomata are in the xerophyte diagram. Are they on the surface like the mesophyte, or somewhere else? What do you think that does to water loss?' WAIT 10 seconds for group discussion before moving on. With 2 minutes remaining, say: 'Finish plotting your last point and add a title to your graph. Make sure your axes are labelled with units.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Parallel Data Sources (Quantitative + Structural): learners process numerical potometer data at the same time as structural diagram data. This dual-channel approach is deliberate — the graph shows THAT transpiration rate changes with conditions, and the adaptation card shows HOW plants structurally respond to those pressures. Holding both simultaneously prepares learners to make the structure-function connection in the Explain Phase.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks 3 specific things during circulation: (1) Is the y-axis labelled with a unit (mm or mm/min)? (2) Are points connected with a smooth line or dot-to-dot? (3) In the comparison table, are learners writing structural differences (thick cuticle, sunken stomata) or only surface-appearance differences (green vs. grey)? Groups still writing only appearance differences need a targeted prompt: 'Look at the cross-section — what is different about the INSIDE of the two leaves?'

framework.learnerExperience: Groups present their graphs to the class (2 minutes per group — teacher selects 2 contrasting groups). The class identifies: which condition produced the steepest gradient (highest rate), which produced the shallowest (lowest rate), and whether there are any anomalous data points. Learners then participate in a structured Claim-Evidence-Reasoning (CER) writing task: each learner writes one CER statement in their notebook connecting the potometer data to the Kericho wilting phenomenon. Example structure: 'Claim: [state which factor most increased transpiration]. Evidence: [cite specific data point from graph]. Reasoning: [explain the stomatal mechanism and link to wilting].' After individual writing, teacher facilitates a class CER gallery — 3 volunteers read their CER aloud. The class then transitions to explaining xerophyte adaptations using the Adaptation Card, completing a 'structure → function → benefit in dry habitat' table for 4 adaptations.
framework.teacherMoves: Invite Group 1 to hold up their graph: 'Group 1, tell us — which condition gave you the fastest bubble movement? Point to that line on your graph.' After response, ask the class: 'Does Group 2's graph show the same trend? [Cold-call Group 2 reporter] — do you agree or disagree, and what does your data say?' WAIT 10 seconds. After both groups report, say: 'So the data suggests that [name the factor] increases transpiration rate. Now I want everyone to write a CER statement. Your CLAIM is the factor. Your EVIDENCE is a number from your graph. Your REASONING must include the word STOMATA and the word WILTING. You have 3 minutes.' WAIT 3 minutes. Cold-call 3 learners to read CER — choose one strong, one developing, one who needs support. After the third reading, say: 'Notice how [learner name] connected the data to the stomata opening — that is exactly what scientists do when they use evidence to explain a phenomenon.' Transition: 'Now — if the Kericho farmer moved her maize farm to Turkana, would the plant survive? Why or why not? Turn and talk.' WAIT 15 seconds. Then say: 'This is where our xerophyte adaptations come in. Let's complete the structure-function table.' Guide completion of the table row by row, cold-calling one learner per row (4 rows = 4 cold-calls).
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Claim-Evidence-Reasoning (CER) Framework: CER is a disciplinary literacy strategy that trains learners to distinguish observation from interpretation and to cite data as the basis of scientific claims. By requiring the words 'stomata' and 'wilting' in the Reasoning, the teacher ensures that the mechanistic explanation is always tied back to the anchoring phenomenon rather than remaining abstract.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect 6 CER notebooks at random at the end of the Explain Phase. Check: (1) Does the Evidence section contain a specific number from the graph (not just 'the graph showed more')? (2) Does the Reasoning mention both stomatal opening AND water potential/transpiration pull? (3) Is the wilting phenomenon explicitly named? Learners who meet all 3 criteria are progressing at standard. Those missing the mechanistic link in Reasoning need targeted feedback before Lesson 7.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to the class Driving Question Board (a large poster or section of the board divided into 'Questions We Have,' 'Evidence We Have Gathered,' and 'Things We Now Understand'). Working individually, each learner writes on a sticky note (or a cut piece of paper): one thing they now understand that they did not before this lesson, and one new question this lesson has raised for them. Sticky notes are placed in the appropriate columns. The class does a 'gallery read' — 60 seconds of silent reading of each other's new questions — and learners place a small tick on any question they share. The teacher circles the top 2–3 most-ticked new questions aloud.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'We started this unit with the question: How does water get from the soil all the way to the top leaves of a tall maize plant — and why does the plant sometimes lose water faster than it can take it up? Look at our Driving Question Board. What have we answered today?' Pause — WAIT 10 seconds — then say: 'Write on your sticky note: one thing you NOW understand, and one NEW question this lesson raised. You have 2 minutes.' WAIT 2 minutes. Invite learners to place their notes on the board. Say: 'Take 60 seconds to read what your classmates wrote — quietly.' After 60 seconds: 'Put a tick on any question you also have.' Count ticks aloud on the top questions and say: 'These are the questions we still need to investigate. Look — [read a question about phloem transport or sugar movement if it appears] — does that remind you of our key inquiry question about manufactured food substances? That will be central in Lesson 7.' Cold-call 2 learners: 'What is the most important thing you now understand about why the maize wilts at midday?'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Question Sorting on the DQB: the DQB is a metacognitive tool that makes the progression of understanding visible and communal. By physically moving questions from 'We Have' to 'We Understand,' learners experience the incremental nature of scientific explanation and maintain motivation to fill remaining gaps. The tick-voting system surfaces genuine shared curiosity rather than teacher-directed topics.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher photographs the updated DQB at the end of the lesson (or notes key sticky note content). Check: (1) Are the 'Things We Now Understand' statements mechanistic (e.g., 'High light causes stomata to open more so more water evaporates') or surface-level (e.g., 'Sunny weather causes wilting')? (2) Do new questions point forward to phloem transport, mineral uptake, or guard cell mechanisms — indicating readiness for Lessons 7–8? (3) Are any learners placing notes in the wrong column — e.g., putting a question in 'Things We Understand'? These learners may need a brief individual check.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners retrieve their cumulative plant-water-movement diagram begun in Lesson 1 and revised in each subsequent lesson. Today's revision adds two new components: (1) an 'Environmental Factors Arrow' beside the leaf, with labels showing how light, temperature, wind, and humidity each affect the size of the stomatal opening and therefore the transpiration rate — learners draw larger stomata apertures next to the 'high light/high temp/wind' labels and smaller ones next to 'low light/high humidity'; (2) a small inset box in the corner of their model labelled 'Xerophyte Comparison' showing a simplified sisal leaf cross-section with sunken stomata and thick cuticle, with an arrow annotation explaining how this structure reduces the transpiration rate that the main diagram shows can exceed uptake. Learners write one 'model update sentence' below their diagram explaining what new mechanism they added and why.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute learners' cumulative model diagrams (or ask them to open to the diagram page in their notebooks). Say: 'Your model has grown over six lessons. Today we are adding the environmental control layer — the part that explains WHY the Kericho farmer's maize wilts at 2pm and not at 7am. Add the Environmental Factors Arrow now. Draw at least two stomata — one wide open labelled HIGH LIGHT/WIND, one nearly closed labelled NIGHT/HIGH HUMIDITY.' WAIT 3 minutes for drawing. Circulate and check that learners are drawing arrows in the correct direction (factors → stomata → transpiration rate → potential wilting). Then say: 'Now add your Xerophyte Comparison inset. It does not need to be large — a thumbnail sketch with three labels is enough.' WAIT 2 minutes. Finally: 'Write your model update sentence at the bottom. It must include the phrase TRANSPIRATION RATE and either WILTING or ADAPTATION.' Cold-call 3 learners to read their update sentence aloud. Close by saying: 'Your model now shows the whole water journey AND the environmental brakes and accelerators. In Lesson 7 we will add the return journey — how sugars manufactured in the leaf travel back DOWN to feed the roots and the maize cob.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision (Progressive Diagramming): requiring learners to add to — rather than redraw — their model makes conceptual growth tangible and prevents compartmentalisation of lesson-by-lesson content. The 'model update sentence' forces metacognitive articulation: the learner must name what changed and why, which deepens encoding of the new mechanism.
framework.formativeAssessment: As learners draw, teacher looks for 3 indicators of understanding: (1) Are the environmental factor arrows pointing FROM the environment TO the stomata (correct causal direction) rather than the reverse? (2) Is the xerophyte inset showing REDUCED stomatal aperture or SUNKEN stomata — not just a different leaf shape? (3) Does the model update sentence use 'transpiration rate' as a variable that can be high or low, not just as a synonym for 'water loss'? Learners whose models show all three are meeting the standard. The model update sentence serves as an exit-ticket proxy — teacher collects 5 notebooks at random to review before Lesson 7.

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
1. When learners presented their graphs, did they cite specific data values (e.g., 'the bubble moved 18 mm/min in sunlight versus 6 mm/min in shade') or did they describe trends in vague terms? What questioning would prompt more precise data citation next time?
2. Did the CER writing task reveal whether learners can distinguish between the CAUSE (environmental factor → stomatal opening) and the EFFECT (transpiration rate exceeding uptake → wilting)? Which learners conflated these, and how will you address this before Lesson 7?
3. Were learners able to transfer their understanding of transpiration rate to the xerophyte context, or did they treat the two parts of the lesson (potometer data and adaptations) as unrelated topics? What connection-making question could you add to bridge them more explicitly?
4. Looking at the DQB after today, do learners' new questions show readiness to investigate phloem transport and translocation in Lesson 7, or are there still unresolved questions about xylem and transpiration that need revisiting?
5. How effectively did the cumulative model revision capture the difference between a mesophyte's and a xerophyte's transpiration control — and if a learner showed you only their model inset, would you be able to assess whether they understood the structural-functional link?

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners observed graphed potometer data showing that high light intensity, elevated temperature, increased wind speed, and low humidity each produced a steeper gradient (faster bubble movement = higher transpiration rate); they also observed cross-section diagrams and, where available, actual specimens of sisal and cactus showing sunken stomata, thickened cuticles, and reduced leaf surface area compared with maize and sukuma wiki leaves.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Environmental factors — especially light intensity and wind — accelerate transpiration by causing stomata to open wider or increasing the diffusion gradient for water vapour; xerophytes such as sisal and cactus possess structural adaptations (sunken stomata, thick waxy cuticle, water-storing tissue, reduced leaf area) that dramatically reduce transpiration rate, allowing survival in Kenya's arid regions where water uptake would otherwise be chronically unable to keep pace with water loss.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The Kericho farmer's maize wilts at midday because the combination of high light intensity, peak temperature, and afternoon wind drives transpiration to a rate that exceeds the root's water uptake capacity — the exact conditions the potometer data showed as producing the fastest bubble movement; by late evening, as light intensity and temperature drop and wind decreases, stomata partially close and the transpiration rate falls back below the uptake rate, allowing turgor pressure to be restored and the plant to recover without additional watering.

LESSONS.title: Lesson 7 — Translocation in Phloem: How Sugars Travel from Leaves to Roots and Fruits
LESSONS.aresKeywords: translocation, phloem, source-sink model, ring-barking, sucrose transport

slo.purpose: Learners investigate how manufactured sugars and amino acids move through phloem from leaves (sources) to roots, fruits, and storage organs (sinks), using ring-barking and aphid stylet evidence to explain translocation in Kenyan crop plants.
slo.knowledge:
• Describe translocation as the movement of sucrose and amino acids through phloem sieve tubes from source to sink
• Explain the source-to-sink model using examples from sugarcane, sweet potato, and maize grown in Kenya
• Interpret evidence from ring-barking and aphid stylet experiments to confirm phloem as the tissue responsible for translocation
slo.skills:
• Analyse and interpret experimental evidence (ring-barking, aphid stylet data) to construct explanations about translocation
• Apply the source-sink model to predict the direction of phloem transport in different Kenyan crop plants under varying conditions
slo.attitudes:
• Appreciate that scientific understanding of translocation directly supports Kenyan farmers in improving yields of sugarcane, sweet potato, and maize
• Show intellectual curiosity by questioning how the plant coordinates sugar distribution across its organs
slo.keyInquiry: How are manufactured food substances transported in plants?
slo.purposeInStoryline: Having established in earlier lessons how water and minerals move upward through xylem (driven by transpiration), learners now investigate the complementary system — phloem translocation — which moves sugars downward and laterally from photosynthesising leaves to growing roots, ripening fruits, and storage organs, completing the full transport picture needed to answer the driving question.
slo.safetyNotes: No specific chemical hazards. If using fresh sugarcane or sweet potato stems for class observation, ensure knives are handled only by the teacher. Remind learners to wash hands after handling plant material.

LESSONS.overview:
This lesson addresses the second major transport pathway in plants: the movement of photosynthetic products (primarily sucrose and amino acids) through phloem. While the first six lessons built understanding of water uptake, xylem transport, and transpiration — directly linked to the wilting phenomenon in the Kericho farmer's maize field — this lesson completes the transport picture by asking: once the leaves make food, where does it go and how does it get there? Learners begin by predicting what would happen if a ring of bark were removed from a sugarcane stem, then examine two classic pieces of experimental evidence: the ring-barking (girdling) experiment, which shows that removing the bark (containing phloem) stops downward sugar transport, causing a sugary swelling above the ring; and the aphid stylet technique, where feeding aphids are anaesthetised and their mouthparts (stylets) are severed, allowing phloem sap to ooze out for analysis, confirming that phloem carries sucrose and amino acids under pressure.

Using these evidence sources, learners construct the source-to-sink model: leaves producing sucrose through photosynthesis are 'sources'; roots, fruits, seeds, and storage organs that consume or store sugars are 'sinks'. In Kenyan contexts, the sugarcane factory towns of Mumias and Chemelil depend on efficient translocation loading sucrose into cane stems, while sweet potato farmers in Western Kenya rely on translocation filling storage roots with starch derived from translocated sucrose. Maize cobs (the grain) are powerful sinks that compete with roots for photosynthate during grain-fill — a concept that directly connects to why a heavily cob-loaded maize plant may show root stress. This cross-connection reinforces the driving question: the plant's water-transport system (xylem, transpiration) and its food-transport system (phloem, translocation) are interdependent, and understanding both helps explain the wilting-and-recovery cycle the Kericho farmer observes.

By the end of the lesson, learners will have revised their cumulative plant transport model to include bidirectional transport: upward water and minerals in xylem, and source-to-sink sucrose in phloem. This positions Lesson 8 to synthesise all evidence into a final, comprehensive explanation of the driving question.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown a diagram of a sugarcane stem and asked to predict, in writing in their science notebooks, what will happen to the stem above and below if a complete ring of bark (about 2 cm wide) is removed from the middle of a healthy, leafy sugarcane stem. They record their predictions using the sentence stem: 'I predict that above the ring _______ will happen because _______, and below the ring _______ will happen because _______.' After 2 minutes of individual writing, learners share predictions with a shoulder partner (Think-Pair-Share) and notice whether their predictions differ.
framework.teacherMoves: Display a large labelled diagram of a sugarcane stem cross-section on the board, with bark, phloem, cambium, xylem, and pith labelled. Say: 'You are a farmer in Mumias — Kenya's sugarcane region. You accidentally cut a ring of bark all the way around one of your mature sugarcane stems. The leaves are still green and healthy. In your notebook, predict: what happens above and below that ring over the next two weeks?' Allow 2 minutes of silent individual writing (WAIT TIME). Then cold-call 3 learners — one who predicts swelling above, one who predicts nothing changes, one who is uncertain — and record all three predictions verbatim on the board under 'Our Predictions'. Say: 'We are NOT deciding who is right yet. We are collecting our thinking so we can test it.' Do NOT confirm or deny any prediction.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Anchored Prediction Writing: Learners commit predictions to writing before seeing evidence, activating prior knowledge about bark, xylem, and phloem from Lessons 5–6. Recording divergent predictions publicly creates cognitive tension that motivates close observation in the next phase.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans notebooks during the 2-minute writing period — look for whether learners distinguish between xylem (inside wood, carries water) and phloem (in bark, carries food). Learners who conflate the two tissues will need targeted support during the Explain phase. Note: if fewer than half the class mention 'food' or 'sugars' in their prediction, plan to add a brief recap question during Observe Phase.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners examine two evidence sources in sequence. First, they read a structured one-page evidence card (prepared by teacher) describing a ring-barking (girdling) experiment: a Kenya Agricultural and Livestock Research Organisation (KALRO) researcher removed a 2 cm ring of bark from a mature sugarcane stem; after 14 days, a swollen, sticky bulge formed just above the ring, the tissue below the ring became starchy and weak, and analysis showed the bulge contained high concentrations of sucrose. Below the ring, roots grew poorly. Learners annotate the diagram on the evidence card with arrows showing where sugars accumulated and where they were blocked. Second, learners examine a data table from an aphid stylet experiment: aphids feeding on a tomato plant (grown in Nairobi greenhouses) were flash-anaesthetised with CO₂ and their stylets severed; the exuding sap was collected and analysed, showing sucrose (120 mM), amino acids (40 mM), potassium ions (80 mM), and only traces of xylem solutes. Learners answer two guided questions on the evidence card before class discussion.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute evidence cards face-down. Say: 'You now have two pieces of real experimental evidence. Scientists at KALRO and plant physiologists worldwide have used these experiments for over 60 years. Your job is to be a scientist — read carefully, annotate the diagram, and answer the two questions at the bottom. You have 7 minutes. Work silently first.' (WAIT TIME: 7 minutes silent reading and annotation.) After 7 minutes, say: 'Now discuss with your table group: what does the ring-barking result tell us about which tissue — xylem or phloem — carries sugars? You have 2 minutes.' Cold-call 2 table groups to report. Listen for the key idea: sugars accumulate above the ring because phloem in the bark was removed, blocking downward transport; xylem inside the wood was undamaged so water still moved upward. Ask the whole class: 'What does the aphid stylet data tell us that the ring-barking experiment alone cannot?' (WAIT TIME: 12 seconds.) Cold-call 2 learners. Target answer: aphid data tells us the actual composition of phloem sap — sucrose and amino acids — and shows it is under positive pressure (sap oozes out without the plant pumping it).
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Two-Source Evidence Triangulation: Learners examine two independent lines of evidence (ring-barking and aphid stylet) that converge on the same conclusion. This mirrors how real scientists build confidence in a claim — no single experiment is sufficient. By explicitly comparing what each experiment can and cannot tell us, learners practise evaluating evidence quality, a core NGSS Science and Engineering Practice.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect annotated evidence cards at the end of the phase. Look for: (1) arrows correctly showing sucrose accumulating above the ring and being blocked from moving below; (2) identification of phloem (not xylem) as the blocked tissue; (3) in the aphid question, learners noting that sucrose and amino acids — not water and minerals — dominate phloem sap. Common error to watch: learners may confuse xylem and phloem directions — if more than 30% of cards show arrows going the wrong way, address this explicitly at the start of the Explain Phase.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners construct the source-to-sink model in three steps. Step 1: They categorise plant organs as 'source' or 'sink' using a Kenya Crops Card Sort — cards showing: mature maize leaf (source), maize cob filling with grain (sink), sweet potato storage root (sink), young unfolded maize leaf (sink), sugarcane stem storing sucrose (sink), germinating bean seed in Rift Valley (sink), and actively photosynthesising sugarcane leaf (source). Learners sort cards on their desks and justify each decision. Step 2: Learners draw a simple diagram of a sweet potato plant (leaves, stem, storage root) and add arrows showing the direction of translocation, labelling the source, sink, phloem, sucrose, and amino acids. Step 3: Learners write a 3-sentence explanation answering: 'How does the sucrose made in a sugarcane leaf in Mumias end up stored in the cane stem?'
framework.teacherMoves: Introduce the source-sink model with a brief analogy: 'Think of a maize plant like a Safaricom M-Pesa network. The leaves are the agents depositing money (sucrose) into the system. The roots, fruits, and storage organs are the customers withdrawing money (sucrose) for their own needs. The phloem is the mobile network — the pathway money travels through.' Distribute the Kenya Crops Card Sort. Allow 3 minutes for sorting (WAIT TIME). Then cold-call one learner to justify why the 'young unfolded maize leaf' is a SINK, not a source — listen for: 'It is not yet photosynthesising enough to export, it is still importing sugars to grow.' This is a high-value misconception check. Then ask the whole class: 'Can a plant organ ever switch from sink to source?' (WAIT TIME: 12 seconds.) Cold-call 2 learners — target answer: yes, a maize leaf starts as a sink when young, becomes a source when mature. Say: 'Now draw your sweet potato diagram. You have 4 minutes.' Circulate and look at diagrams. Prompt struggling learners: 'Where is photosynthesis happening? That is your source. Where is food being stored? That is your sink. Draw an arrow from source to sink through the phloem.' After diagrams, cold-call one learner to read their 3-sentence explanation aloud. Class gives thumbs up/sideways/down on whether the explanation includes: (1) source leaf, (2) phloem pathway, (3) sink storage root.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Card Sort + Diagram Construction + Peer Evaluation: The card sort externalises and challenges learners' intuitive categorisation of plant organs, surfacing the important idea that 'source' and 'sink' are functional (not permanent) labels. Diagram construction requires learners to spatialise the abstract model, and peer evaluation (thumbs) of the 3-sentence explanation provides immediate, low-stakes feedback on explanatory completeness.
framework.formativeAssessment: Circulate during diagram construction — specifically check: (1) arrows point from leaf toward storage root (not the reverse); (2) phloem is labelled on the arrow (not xylem); (3) sucrose/amino acids labelled as the transported substances. During the thumbs peer-evaluation, note which learners give 'sideways' (uncertain) — these learners need follow-up. The 3-sentence written explanation is a key artifact: collect from 5–6 learners (mix of abilities) to review after class.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to the class Driving Question Board displayed at the front of the room. They re-read the driving question: 'How does water get from the soil all the way to the top leaves of a tall maize plant — and why does the plant sometimes lose water faster than it can take it up?' Working individually on a sticky note, each learner writes one sentence explaining how today's lesson on phloem translocation connects to (or extends) their answer to the driving question. They add their sticky note to the DQB in a new section labelled 'Lesson 7: Phloem & Translocation'. The class then identifies two new sub-questions that have emerged: e.g., 'Does drought stress affect phloem translocation as well as xylem transport?' and 'How do sugarcane plants load so much sucrose into the stem — is there an active mechanism?'
framework.teacherMoves: Point to the DQB and say: 'Look at what we have built over 7 lessons. In Lessons 1–4 we explained how water enters roots by osmosis. In Lessons 5–6 we explained how water rises in xylem and is lost through stomata. Today we added the phloem story. How does phloem translocation connect to the wilting maize plant in Kericho? Take 90 seconds — write one sentence on your sticky note.' (WAIT TIME: 90 seconds silence.) As learners post notes, read 3–4 aloud. Then say: 'What NEW questions does today's evidence raise for you? Talk to your partner for 30 seconds.' Cold-call 3 pairs to share new questions. Write the best 2 new questions on the DQB in a different colour marker — say: 'These become questions for Lesson 8, where we bring everything together.' Explicitly link to the phenomenon: 'The Kericho farmer's maize wilts because water loss in xylem outruns water uptake. Now we know: even while water is being lost at the top, phloem is still moving sucrose downward to the roots. How might a wilting plant affect that sugar supply to the roots? That is a question worth investigating.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: DQB Sticky Note Accumulation: The DQB functions as a public, growing record of the class's collaborative understanding. Adding Lesson 7 notes alongside Lessons 1–6 makes the cumulative nature of scientific explanation visible and tangible. Generating new questions from new evidence models how scientific inquiry is self-extending — answers produce new questions.
framework.formativeAssessment: Read all sticky notes as learners post them. Look for: learners who can connect phloem translocation to the phenomenon (e.g., 'While water is being lost from leaves in the heat, phloem is still transporting sugars to keep roots alive'); learners who conflate xylem and phloem (a critical error to address in Lesson 8 synthesis). Notes that only restate facts without connecting to the phenomenon indicate learners have not yet achieved explanatory sensemaking — these learners should be grouped with stronger peers during Lesson 8.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners retrieve their cumulative plant transport model diagram, which they have been revising since Lesson 3. This model currently shows: root hair cells, osmosis into root, xylem vessels in stem, transpiration pull, stomata on leaf, and guard cell control. Today they add the phloem layer: they draw phloem sieve tubes running parallel to xylem (but labelled separately), add arrows showing sucrose moving from the mature leaf (source) downward to the root (sink) and toward any fruit/storage organ shown, label the transported substances (sucrose, amino acids), and add a text box that reads: 'Phloem transport = TRANSLOCATION. Direction: source → sink. Driven by pressure flow (high pressure at source, low pressure at sink).' Learners also annotate their model with a Kenya crop example: 'In Mumias sugarcane, sucrose loaded into phloem in leaves is transported to the stem, where it is stored — this is what sugar factories extract.'
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Take out your cumulative model. You have been building this since Lesson 3. Today it gets its most important new layer — the phloem system. You have 5 minutes to add phloem, arrows, labels, and your Kenya crop annotation. Make your model tell the FULL story of transport in a plant.' (WAIT TIME: 5 minutes.) Circulate actively — look at 6–8 models. Ask targeted prompts: 'Show me which direction your phloem arrows are going — and why.' 'Where is your source? Where is your sink? Can I see both in the model?' 'If I covered up all your labels, could someone else look at your model and understand both xylem AND phloem transport?' At 4 minutes, give a 1-minute warning. At close, ask one volunteer to hold up their model and describe it to the class in 60 seconds — class listens and identifies one strength and one thing to add. End with: 'In Lesson 8, you will use this complete model to write your final explanation of the Kericho farmer's observation. Make sure your model is ready.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision: Returning to the same model diagram across all 8 lessons makes learning progression visible and reinforces that scientific models are revised as new evidence accumulates — not replaced wholesale. The requirement to annotate with a real Kenyan crop example (sugarcane, Mumias) grounds the abstract model in authentic, local agricultural context and strengthens transfer of understanding.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher photographs or quickly sketches observations from 4–5 models during circulation. Key indicators of proficiency: (1) phloem arrows pointing in the correct direction (source to sink, generally leaf to root/fruit); (2) phloem drawn as a SEPARATE vessel from xylem, not the same tube; (3) both sucrose/amino acids (phloem) and water/minerals (xylem) labelled and distinguished; (4) a source and at least one sink are identified and labelled. Learners whose models still show only xylem (no phloem added) must be flagged for immediate support at the start of Lesson 8.

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
1. During the Predict Phase, what range of prior knowledge about phloem and bark did learners reveal in their ring-barking predictions — and how did this inform your pacing during the Explain Phase?
2. Were learners able to distinguish phloem from xylem in both direction and content (sucrose/amino acids vs. water/minerals) by the end of the lesson — or did confusion between the two tissues persist? What specific language or diagrams helped most?
3. How effectively did the Kenya Crops Card Sort surface the idea that 'source' and 'sink' are functional, changeable labels rather than permanent organ identities — and which cards caused the most productive disagreement?
4. Looking at the cumulative plant transport models collected at the end, what proportion of learners successfully integrated phloem translocation into their existing xylem-transpiration model as a parallel but distinct system — and what gap in instruction might explain any remaining confusion?
5. How well did learners connect phloem translocation back to the Kericho farmer's wilting maize phenomenon on the DQB sticky notes — and what question or prompt would help the weakest connectors make this link explicit in Lesson 8's synthesis?

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners observed two pieces of experimental evidence: (1) ring-barking data showing sucrose accumulation above a bark-removal ring in sugarcane, proving phloem carries sugars downward; and (2) aphid stylet sap analysis confirming phloem sap contains sucrose and amino acids under positive pressure.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Translocation is the movement of sucrose and amino acids through phloem sieve tubes from sources (photosynthesising leaves) to sinks (roots, storage organs, fruits, growing regions). The direction of phloem flow is from high-sugar-concentration source to low-sugar-concentration sink, illustrated by Kenyan crops: sugarcane (leaf to stem storage), sweet potato (leaf to storage root), and maize (leaf to grain cob).
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The phloem system explains how the Kericho farmer's maize plant, even while losing water rapidly through transpiration during afternoon heat, simultaneously distributes photosynthetic sugars to roots and developing cobs. This reveals that the plant operates two parallel transport systems: xylem (water and minerals, leaf-pulled upward by transpiration) and phloem (sucrose and amino acids, pressure-driven from source to sink) — and that wilting from water deficit in the xylem pathway does not immediately shut down phloem translocation, which helps explain how roots remain supplied and the plant can recover when evening temperatures reduce transpiration.

LESSONS.title: Lesson 8 — Model Revision, Synthesis & Final Explanation: Solving the Mystery of the Wilting Maize Plant

slo.purpose: Learners synthesise all evidence gathered across Lessons 1–7 to construct a complete, evidence-based explanation of the anchoring phenomenon and finalise their cumulative transport model.
slo.knowledge:
• Describe the complete pathway of water from soil through root hair cells, xylem vessels, mesophyll cells, stomata, and into the atmosphere
• Explain how transpiration pull, root pressure, osmosis, and cohesion–tension work together to move water up a tall maize plant
• Distinguish the xylem pathway (water and mineral salts, upward) from the phloem pathway (sugars, bidirectional) and explain why wilting occurs when transpiration rate exceeds water uptake rate
slo.skills:
• Revise and annotate a cumulative diagrammatic model of plant transport to reflect all evidence gathered across the sub-strand
• Construct a written evidence-based scientific explanation using the Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) framework to answer the driving question
slo.attitudes:
• Appreciate that scientific understanding is built incrementally through repeated observation, prediction, and revision of models
• Value the relevance of plant biology to the lived experiences of Kenyan farmers, recognising that science explains everyday agricultural observations
slo.keyInquiry: How does water get from the soil all the way to the top leaves of a tall maize plant — and why does the plant sometimes lose water faster than it can take it up?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This final lesson closes the sub-strand storyline by returning to the farmer in Kericho whose maize plants wilt in the afternoon and recover by evening; learners now have all the conceptual tools to explain every part of that observation and to compare their final model with the naive model they drew in Lesson 1, demonstrating measurable growth in understanding.
slo.safetyNotes: No specific hazards. Lesson is entirely discussion, writing, and drawing-based. Ensure learners handle pens and rulers safely during model drawing.

LESSONS.overview:
Lesson 8 is the capstone lesson for Sub-Strand 2.2: Transport in Plants. After seven lessons of building evidence about osmosis, root hair cell structure, xylem and phloem anatomy, transpiration, stomatal control, cohesion–tension, root pressure, and translocation, learners are now equipped to return to the anchoring phenomenon — the Kericho farmer's well-watered maize plants wilting at midday and recovering at dusk — and construct a complete, scientifically accurate explanation.

The lesson begins by asking learners to make a final prediction about what a complete explanation must include, activating prior knowledge. In the Observe phase, learners revisit their Driving Question Board (DQB) from Lessons 1–7, matching questions posted early in the sub-strand to evidence gathered in subsequent lessons.
