In the Explain phase, learners use the Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) framework to write a structured final explanation of the phenomenon, working in pairs before sharing with the class. The DQB phase involves formally closing answered questions and identifying any remaining uncertainties as springboards for future learning. Finally, in the Model Building phase, learners compare their Lesson 1 naive model with their final revised model, annotating the differences in a structured comparison, and the teacher facilitates a whole-class gallery walk of final models.

Assessment in this lesson is both formative (CER writing, model annotation quality, DQB participation) and summative (the final written CER explanation serves as the end-of-sub-strand assessment task). The lesson honours Kenya's agricultural context throughout — the maize plant remains central, and learners are encouraged to connect their explanations to decisions a real farmer in Kericho or the Rift Valley might make to reduce wilting stress in their crops.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners open their exercise books and, working individually for 3 minutes, write answers to the prompt: 'Before we explain the wilting maize plant fully, list every mechanism or structure you now know that is involved in getting water from the soil to the top leaf. Then list what you think causes the wilting and what causes the recovery.' Learners then share their lists with a partner and agree on a combined list of at least six mechanisms or structures before the class discussion begins.
framework.teacherMoves: Write the prompt on the board: 'List every mechanism or structure involved in moving water from soil to the top leaf of a maize plant. What causes the wilting? What causes recovery?' Say: 'You have studied this for seven lessons. Before we build our final explanation together, I want you to write down everything you already know — on your own, no talking, for three minutes.' START a 3-minute timer visibly. After 3 minutes say: 'Now share your list with your partner. Agree on a combined list with at least six items. You have two minutes.' After pair time, cold-call 4 different pairs: 'Amina and Kamau — give me two items from your list.' Record all contributed items on the board in two columns: STRUCTURES and MECHANISMS. Do NOT correct yet — this is a prediction. Say: 'We are going to use all of these today. By the end of this lesson, your final explanation should include every one of these.' Allow 10–15 seconds WAIT TIME after each cold-call before prompting.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Activating Prior Knowledge through Individual then Paired Brainstorm: This strategy surfaces what learners already hold in memory before instruction, making implicit knowledge explicit and creating cognitive readiness to integrate it into a coherent explanation. The two-column board display (STRUCTURES vs MECHANISMS) scaffolds later model-building by distinguishing anatomical features from physiological processes.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans written lists during partner talk: look for whether learners include all seven key items — root hair cells, osmosis, xylem vessels, cohesion–tension, transpiration pull, stomata, and phloem. Flag any pairs whose lists have fewer than four items for targeted support during the Explain phase. Check whether learners distinguish wilting (transpiration > uptake, stomata open wide in heat/light) from recovery (stomata close at dusk, transpiration slows, root pressure continues, water balance restores).

framework.learnerExperience: Learners conduct a structured 'DQB Revisit.' Each learner receives their personal copy of the questions they posted or contributed to the Driving Question Board in Lesson 1 (or the teacher has preserved the class DQB on the wall). Working in groups of three, learners go through each question on the DQB and write next to it: (a) which lesson provided evidence to answer it, (b) a one-sentence answer, and (c) whether they feel the question is fully answered, partially answered, or still open. Groups record this in a table in their books. If a physical DQB wall exists, each group receives sticky notes to mark answered questions with a GREEN dot and unanswered ones with a YELLOW dot.
framework.teacherMoves: Direct learners to their DQB records. Say: 'In Lesson 1, you asked questions about how the maize plant moves water. You have spent seven lessons collecting evidence. Now we are going to see how many of those questions we can actually answer.' Distribute the DQB Revisit Table (or draw it on the board: columns — OUR QUESTION | WHICH LESSON ANSWERED IT | ONE-SENTENCE ANSWER | FULLY/PARTLY/STILL OPEN). Give groups 6 minutes to complete the table. Circulate and listen. Prompt groups who are stuck: 'Which lesson did we look at stomata and light? What did we observe about guard cells and potassium ions?' After group work, cold-call 3 groups to share one answered question each. Ask: 'What evidence from our experiments or readings convinced you that this question is answered?' WAIT TIME: 12 seconds after each question before accepting responses. End by asking: 'Are there any questions we still cannot fully answer? What would we need to investigate to answer them?' Record open questions on the board under 'Still Wondering.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: DQB Audit (Evidence Mapping): This strategy requires learners to trace each question back to a specific lesson and a specific piece of evidence, building metacognitive awareness of how knowledge accumulates across a unit. By categorising questions as fully/partly/still open, learners practise the scientific habit of distinguishing what is known from what remains uncertain — a core practice in NGSS Science and Engineering Practice 6 (Constructing Explanations) and Practice 8 (Obtaining and Evaluating Evidence).
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks that each group's table references specific evidence (e.g., 'Lesson 4 — the celery stalk experiment showed water moves up xylem' rather than vague statements like 'we learnt about xylem'). Flag any group that cannot connect an answer to a specific lesson activity — these learners may need the summary chart in the Explain phase. Monitor whether learners correctly identify that stomatal control (Lesson 6) is the key mechanism explaining both the afternoon wilting and the evening recovery.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners construct a final written explanation of the anchoring phenomenon using the Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) framework. Each learner writes individually in their exercise book: CLAIM — a one-sentence answer to the driving question; EVIDENCE — at least five specific pieces of evidence from experiments, diagrams, or readings across the sub-strand; REASONING — a paragraph connecting each piece of evidence to the claim and explaining the mechanisms involved. The explanation must specifically address: (1) how water enters root hair cells, (2) how water travels up the xylem of the maize stem, (3) why the maize plant wilts at midday, and (4) why it recovers without additional watering by evening. Learners then share their CER with a partner for peer feedback using two stars and a wish.
framework.teacherMoves: Write the CER scaffold on the board: 'CLAIM: In one sentence, answer — How does water get from the soil to the top leaves, and why does the maize plant wilt and recover?' / 'EVIDENCE: List at least 5 specific pieces of evidence from our lessons.' / 'REASONING: Connect each piece of evidence to your claim. Explain the mechanisms in order.' Say: 'This is your final explanation for this sub-strand. A scientist does not just say what happens — they use evidence and they explain why and how. You are now going to do exactly that.' Allow 8 minutes for individual writing. Circulate and read over shoulders. Prompt learners who write only one or two evidence points: 'What did we observe about guard cells in Lesson 6? Can you include that?' After writing, say: 'Share your CER with your partner. Give two stars — two things they explained well — and one wish — one thing they could add or clarify.' Allow 3 minutes for peer feedback. Cold-call 2 learners to read their CLAIM aloud. Ask the class: 'Does this claim answer both parts of the driving question — the upward movement AND the wilting/recovery?' WAIT TIME: 15 seconds before taking responses. Affirm strong claims and coach weaker ones by asking: 'Does your claim mention what happens differently in the afternoon versus the evening?'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) Writing: CER is a structured scientific writing framework (NGSS Science and Engineering Practice 6) that requires learners to explicitly state a conclusion, support it with specific data or observations, and articulate the logical and mechanistic link between the two. In this context it forces learners to synthesise osmosis, xylem transport, transpiration, stomatal control, and phloem translocation into a single coherent narrative — mirroring the work of a plant physiologist constructing a scientific argument. Peer feedback with 'two stars and a wish' builds metacognitive revision skills.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reads 4–6 CER responses during peer feedback time. Strong responses will: (1) mention osmosis and water potential gradient as the entry mechanism at root hair cells; (2) name cohesion–tension and transpiration pull as the force driving upward movement through xylem; (3) explain that high temperature and light intensity at midday cause stomata to open wide, increasing transpiration beyond the rate of water uptake, causing turgor loss and wilting; (4) explain that in the evening, reduced light causes guard cells to lose potassium ions, stomata close, transpiration rate falls, root pressure continues supplying water, turgor is restored, and the plant stands upright. Flag any CER that omits the stomatal control mechanism as requiring follow-up discussion.

framework.learnerExperience: The class returns to the full class DQB displayed on the wall (or projected on screen). The teacher leads a whole-class ceremony of 'closing' the driving question: learners vote on whether the class can now fully answer the main driving question. Each learner writes a final one-sentence answer to the driving question on a sticky note and places it on the DQB next to the original driving question posted in Lesson 1. The class also identifies two or three questions that remain open or that have spawned new curiosity — these are moved to a 'Questions for Future Investigation' section of the board. Finally, learners add one new question generated by today's synthesis that connects to future learning (e.g., 'How does transport in animals compare to transport in plants?' — bridging to Sub-Strand 3.2).
framework.teacherMoves: Stand at the DQB. Say: 'We started this sub-strand with one big question: How does water get from the soil to the top leaves of a tall maize plant — and why does the plant sometimes lose water faster than it can take it up? Today, I want to know: can we answer it? Write your one-sentence final answer on a sticky note — you have 2 minutes.' After 2 minutes, invite all learners to post their sticky notes on the DQB around the original driving question. Read 3–4 aloud. Say: 'Look at all these answers. In Lesson 1, many of us said things like the plant just absorbs water — we did not know about xylem, cohesion–tension, or guard cells. Look how far our thinking has come.' Then point to the 'Still Wondering' section from the Observe phase: 'Are there questions we still cannot fully answer?' Accept 2–3 contributions. Move those sticky notes to a 'Future Investigations' corner. Ask: 'Now that we understand transport in plants, what question does this make you ask about transport in animals?' Cold-call 3 learners. WAIT TIME: 10 seconds. Record bridge questions on the board. Say: 'These questions will drive our next sub-strand.' Leave the DQB visible for the Model Building phase.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: DQB Closure and Bridge-Building: Formally closing the DQB by having every learner contribute a final answer sticky note creates a visible, communal record of collective knowledge growth. Identifying 'still open' questions and 'bridge questions' to the next sub-strand models authentic scientific practice — no investigation fully closes all questions, and new questions always emerge. This strategy also develops metacognitive awareness by making learners conscious of the arc of their own learning from Lesson 1 to Lesson 8.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reads the sticky-note final answers to assess class-wide understanding. Look for: completeness (does the answer address both the upward journey and the wilting/recovery?), accuracy of mechanisms named, and use of scientific vocabulary (xylem, transpiration, stomata, osmosis, turgor). If more than one-third of sticky notes lack mention of stomatal control as the explanation for wilting, address this before the Model Building phase with a brief 60-second whole-class clarification: 'Everyone — the KEY to the wilting and recovery is the stomata. Tell your partner in one sentence why.' Cold-call 2 pairs.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners retrieve their Lesson 1 naive model (the initial diagram they drew of how water moves in a maize plant) and place it beside a new full page in their exercise books. They draw their FINAL REVISED MODEL of transport in plants, which must include: the soil-to-root pathway (osmosis, root hair cells, root pressure), the xylem pathway from root to stem to leaf (cohesion–tension, transpiration pull, labelled xylem vessel), the stomata on the leaf surface (open in afternoon, closed in evening — show both states), the phloem pathway for sugar translocation from leaves down to roots and developing maize cobs, and labels explaining what drives each stage. After drawing, learners write a 'Model Comparison Note' below the two models: three things that changed between their Lesson 1 model and their final model, and one thing they still find confusing. The lesson ends with a 3-minute gallery walk where learners view two neighbouring pairs' final models and give one written compliment on a sticky note placed on the model.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute or direct learners to retrieve their Lesson 1 model. Say: 'Place your Lesson 1 model on the left side of your book. You are going to draw your FINAL MODEL on the right. This model must show the COMPLETE story — water in, water up, water out, sugar down. Every arrow must be labelled with the mechanism that drives it.' Show a checklist on the board: ☐ Root hair cell + osmosis ☐ Xylem vessel + cohesion–tension + transpiration pull ☐ Leaf stomata — open state (afternoon) + closed state (evening) ☐ Phloem vessel + translocation + sugar from leaf to root/cob ☐ Every arrow labelled with driving mechanism. Give learners 7 minutes to draw. Circulate — do not draw for learners, but ask guiding questions: 'What force pulls water up the xylem? Where does that force come from? What happens to that force when the stomata close?' After drawing, say: 'Below your two models, write THREE things that changed between your Lesson 1 model and your final model. What do you know now that you did not know in Lesson 1?' Allow 2 minutes. Then say: 'Stand up. You have 3 minutes to look at the final models of the two pairs nearest you. Leave one sticky-note compliment on each model — something specific they drew or labelled well.' After the gallery walk, close the lesson: 'The farmer in Kericho is watching her maize wilt at midday. She now knows exactly why. And so do you. You have done the work of a plant physiologist this term. Well done.' Collect CER written explanations as the end-of-sub-strand assessment task.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Model Comparison (Lesson 1 vs Final) with Annotated Revision: Asking learners to place their naive model alongside their final model and explicitly articulate what changed is a powerful metacognitive strategy aligned with NGSS Science and Engineering Practice 2 (Developing and Using Models). It makes conceptual growth visible and personal, reinforcing that science is a process of progressive model revision. The gallery walk adds social validation and exposes learners to peer representations that may clarify their own remaining confusions.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher collects CER written explanations as the summative end-of-sub-strand assessment. Assess against four criteria: (1) Accuracy — are all mechanisms named correctly? (2) Completeness — are both xylem and phloem pathways addressed? (3) Phenomenon connection — does the explanation specifically account for the midday wilting and evening recovery of the Kericho farmer's maize? (4) Evidence use — are at least four specific pieces of evidence from lessons cited? For final models, look for: directionality of arrows (xylem upward, phloem bidirectional), labelled stomata in two states, and root hair cell structure. Flag any learner whose final model does not differ meaningfully from their Lesson 1 model for follow-up consolidation support.

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
1. When learners compared their Lesson 1 model to their final model, what specific misconceptions from Lesson 1 were most commonly revised — and were there any misconceptions that persisted into the final model that I should address at the start of Sub-Strand 3.2?
2. Did all learners successfully connect the stomatal closure mechanism to both the wilting (afternoon, stomata open, transpiration > uptake) and the recovery (evening, stomata close, transpiration falls, turgor restored) — or did some CER explanations only address one half of the phenomenon?
3. How effectively did the DQB serve as a tracking tool across all eight lessons? Were learners able to map specific questions to specific lessons and evidence, or did the DQB feel disconnected from daily learning after Lesson 1?
4. Which learners produced the strongest CER explanations, and which struggled most with the Reasoning section (connecting evidence to claim)? What scaffolding could I provide in future units to build CER writing skills progressively from Lesson 1 rather than only at the end?
5. Did the gallery walk of final models generate genuine peer learning, or was it rushed given the 40-minute time constraint? Would this lesson benefit from being extended to two lessons — one for CER writing and DQB closure, and a second dedicated to model comparison and gallery walk assessment?

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners observed — by reviewing their own DQB records, CER writing, and model comparisons — that their understanding of transport in plants has grown from a vague notion of 'roots absorbing water' in Lesson 1 to a detailed mechanistic account involving osmosis, xylem cohesion–tension, transpiration pull, stomatal guard cell regulation, and phloem translocation. They observed that the question of why the Kericho farmer's maize wilts and recovers can now be fully explained using evidence gathered across eight lessons.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Learners synthesised that: (1) water enters root hair cells by osmosis down a water potential gradient; (2) cohesion–tension driven by transpiration pull moves water continuously up xylem vessels; (3) at midday, high light intensity and temperature cause stomata to open wide, evaporation from mesophyll cells accelerates, and transpiration rate exceeds the rate of water uptake — turgor pressure falls and the plant wilts; (4) at dusk, reduced light triggers potassium ion loss from guard cells, stomata close, transpiration slows, root pressure continues supplying water, turgor is restored, and the plant stands upright — without any additional watering; (5) phloem transports sucrose from photosynthesising leaves to roots, growing cobs, and other sink organs in a separate pathway from xylem.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The phenomenon is fully explained: the well-watered Kericho maize plants wilt at midday because the rate of water loss through open stomata (driven by intense midday sun and heat) exceeds the rate at which osmosis and xylem transport can deliver water from the moist soil to the leaves, causing a temporary loss of turgor pressure. By evening, cooler temperatures and reduced light intensity cause guard cells to close the stomata, dramatically reducing transpiration; root pressure and continued osmotic uptake restore water to the leaves, turgor pressure returns, and the plants stand upright — all without additional watering. The soil was never the problem; the rate of water delivery relative to the rate of loss was the issue, and the plant's own stomatal regulation mechanism provided the solution.

FINAL_EXPLANATION.instructions: This Final Explanation assessment asks you to demonstrate everything you have learned across all eight lessons on Transport in Plants. You will answer questions that connect directly to the phenomenon of the wilting maize plants in Kericho — plants that drooped in the afternoon heat even though the soil was still moist, and then recovered by evening without any extra watering. Your answers should use scientific vocabulary accurately, refer to specific structures and processes (osmosis, transpiration, cohesion-tension, translocation, xylem, phloem, stomata, guard cells), and connect evidence from our investigations to explain what you observed. Write in full sentences and paragraphs. Where a question asks you to draw or annotate a diagram, label all relevant structures clearly. Your final explanation should tell a complete story — from water entering the root hairs in the Kericho soil, travelling up through the stem, reaching the uppermost leaf, and being lost to the hot afternoon air — and explain why the plant recovers when conditions cool in the evening. You will also explain how the sugars made in the leaves during photosynthesis travel to the rest of the plant. Read every section carefully before you begin. You have 60 minutes to complete this assessment.

sections.title: SECTION 1: WATER UPTAKE AND MOVEMENT FROM SOIL TO ROOT XYLEM
sections.prompt: The farmer's maize plants have healthy roots surrounded by moist soil in Kericho. Explain in detail how water molecules move from the soil into the root hair cells and then across the root cortex until they reach the xylem vessels at the centre of the root. In your answer: (a) name and explain the process that drives water into the root hair cells, referring to water potential; (b) describe the two pathways water can take across the root cortex (apoplast and symplast); and (c) explain what forces water into the xylem and identify the name given to the pressure that can be measured in roots. Use the maize plant in Kericho as your context throughout.
sections.exemplar:
Water enters the root hair cells of the maize plant by osmosis — the movement of water molecules from a region of higher water potential (the moist Kericho soil solution) to a region of lower water potential (the cytoplasm of the root hair cell, which contains dissolved mineral salts and sugars). The root hair cells have a very large surface area, which maximises the rate of water absorption. Once inside the root hair cell, water can travel across the root cortex by two routes. In the apoplast pathway, water moves through the porous cell walls and intercellular spaces without entering the cytoplasm; this is the faster of the two routes. In the symplast pathway, water moves through the cytoplasm and plasmodesmata from cell to cell, driven by the osmotic gradient that exists because each successive cell closer to the centre has a slightly lower water potential.

When water travelling via the apoplast pathway reaches the endodermis — a single layer of cells surrounding the vascular bundle — it is blocked by the Casparian strip, a band of waxy suberin deposited in the endodermal cell walls. This forces all water to enter the symplast and pass through the endodermal cell membrane, allowing the plant to regulate which mineral ions enter the xylem. Inside the endodermis, mineral ions are actively transported into the xylem, lowering its water potential and drawing water in by osmosis. The accumulation of water in the xylem creates a positive hydrostatic pressure known as root pressure. On cool, humid Kericho evenings, when transpiration is low, root pressure is sufficient to push water a short distance up the stem, which partly explains why the wilted maize plants begin to recover after sunset even without additional watering.

sections.title: SECTION 2: TRANSPORT OF WATER UP THE STEM — THE COHESION-TENSION THEORY
sections.prompt: A tall maize plant in Kericho may reach 2.5 metres in height. Root pressure alone is not strong enough to push water to the topmost leaves. Explain in full how water reaches the upper leaves of the maize plant. In your answer: (a) describe the role of transpiration in creating the 'pull' on the water column; (b) explain the properties of water — cohesion and adhesion — that allow this pull to be transmitted all the way from the leaf to the root; (c) name this explanation as the cohesion-tension theory and state who proposed it; and (d) describe the structure of xylem vessels that makes them well adapted for this function. Include in your answer why the water column does not break even under tension.
sections.exemplar:
Water is transported from the roots to the topmost leaves of the maize plant primarily through the cohesion-tension mechanism, first proposed by Irish scientist Henry Dixon and John Joly in 1894. The driving force begins in the leaves: as water evaporates from the mesophyll cells into the air spaces of the leaf and then diffuses out through the stomata (a process called transpiration), the water potential of the mesophyll cells drops. This causes water to move by osmosis from the xylem vessels in the leaf veins into the mesophyll cells, creating a tension — a negative pressure or 'pull' — at the top of the xylem column.

This tension is transmitted down the entire water column in the xylem because of two key properties of water molecules. Cohesion refers to the strong hydrogen bonds that water molecules form with each other; these bonds keep the water column intact so that when molecules are pulled upward at the top, the entire column moves with them like a chain being pulled from the top link. Adhesion refers to the attraction between water molecules and the cellulose and lignin walls of the xylem vessels, which prevents the column from pulling away from the sides of the tube under tension. The xylem vessels are dead, hollow tubes with lignified, waterproof walls that are reinforced with spiral or ring thickening — this prevents the walls from collapsing inward as the tension increases on a hot Kericho afternoon. Because of cohesion, the water column does not snap even under the very low (negative) pressures created during peak transpiration, provided the xylem remains filled with water and no air bubbles enter the column.

sections.title: SECTION 3: TRANSPIRATION, STOMATA, AND THE AFTERNOON WILTING PHENOMENON
sections.prompt: Return to the central phenomenon: the maize plants in Kericho wilt in the hot afternoon sun even though the soil is still moist. Using your knowledge of transpiration and stomatal control, explain: (a) what transpiration is and identify the main route through which water vapour is lost from the leaf; (b) how environmental factors present in a hot, sunny Kericho afternoon — high temperature, high light intensity, low humidity, and wind — each increase the rate of transpiration; (c) how guard cells control stomatal opening and closing, including the role of potassium ions and turgor pressure; and (d) why wilting occurs when transpiration rate exceeds water uptake rate, and why the plant recovers in the cool evening without additional watering. Draw and annotate a diagram of a stoma and its two guard cells in the open and closed positions.
sections.exemplar:
Transpiration is the loss of water vapour from the aerial parts of a plant, primarily through tiny pores called stomata located mainly on the lower epidermis of leaves. Each stoma is flanked by two guard cells whose shape controls whether the pore is open or closed. On a hot, sunny Kericho afternoon, several environmental factors combine to dramatically increase the rate of transpiration. High temperature increases the kinetic energy of water molecules in the leaf's air spaces, causing faster evaporation; it also reduces the relative humidity of the air, increasing the water vapour concentration gradient between the inside of the leaf and the external atmosphere, which accelerates diffusion outward. High light intensity triggers guard cells to open the stomata fully to allow carbon dioxide in for photosynthesis. Low humidity and wind continuously remove water vapour from the boundary layer of air surrounding the leaf, maintaining a steep diffusion gradient and preventing the air near the stomata from becoming saturated.

Guard cells open and close the stoma by changing their turgor pressure. In bright light, guard cells take up potassium ions (K⁺) by active transport from surrounding epidermal cells. This lowers the water potential inside the guard cells, causing water to enter by osmosis. The increased turgor pressure causes the guard cells to swell; because their inner walls are thicker than their outer walls, they bow outward, opening the stomatal pore. At night or under water stress, potassium ions are pumped out, water leaves by osmosis, the guard cells become flaccid, and the stoma closes.

Wilting occurs when the rate of water loss through transpiration exceeds the rate of water uptake by the roots. On the hot Kericho afternoon, the demand for water at the leaves is so high — driven by intense sun, heat, and dry air — that even though the soil is moist, the xylem pathway cannot deliver water fast enough. The mesophyll cells lose more water than they receive, their turgor pressure drops, and the whole plant droops. By evening, temperatures fall, humidity rises, light intensity drops, guard cells close the stomata, and transpiration slows dramatically. Water uptake from the still-moist soil catches up, turgor pressure is restored in all cells, and the maize plant stands upright again — all without any extra watering from the farmer.

sections.title: SECTION 4: MINERAL SALT TRANSPORT AND THE ROLE OF XYLEM
sections.prompt: The farmer in Kericho applies a fertiliser containing nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K) to her maize field. Explain how these mineral salts — dissolved in the soil water — are absorbed by the roots and transported to the leaves and growing regions of the maize plant. In your answer: (a) distinguish between the processes of osmosis and active transport in the context of mineral uptake; (b) explain why mineral uptake requires energy and identify where this energy comes from; (c) describe how minerals travel through the plant once they are inside the xylem; and (d) state what would happen to the maize plant if the farmer applied too much fertiliser, explaining your answer using the concept of water potential.
sections.exemplar:
When the farmer applies NPK fertiliser to her Kericho maize field, the mineral salts dissolve in the soil water and surround the root hair cells. Unlike water, mineral ions cannot move into the root by osmosis alone because they are often present in higher concentrations inside the root cells than in the soil solution — meaning they must move against their concentration gradient. This requires active transport: specialised carrier proteins embedded in the root hair cell membrane use energy in the form of ATP to pump specific mineral ions (such as nitrate, phosphate, and potassium) from the soil solution into the cytoplasm of the root hair cells. The ATP is produced by cellular respiration in the root cells, which is why waterlogged soils — where oxygen is limited and respiration is reduced — lead to poor mineral uptake and stunted plant growth.

Once mineral ions have entered the root cells, they move across the root cortex via the symplast pathway and eventually pass into the xylem vessels. From there, the upward flow of water driven by transpiration (the transpiration stream) carries the dissolved mineral ions all the way up the stem to the leaves and other growing regions such as the cob and developing grains, where they are used to build proteins, chlorophyll, cell membranes, and other essential molecules. If the farmer applies too much fertiliser, the concentration of dissolved salts in the soil solution rises dramatically, potentially exceeding the solute concentration inside the root hair cells. This means the soil solution now has a lower water potential than the cell cytoplasm, and water will actually move out of the root hair cells by osmosis into the soil — a condition called plasmolysis. The plant will wilt, the roots will be damaged, and the crop yield will fall, despite the farmer's intention to help the plants.

sections.title: SECTION 5: TRANSLOCATION — TRANSPORT OF MANUFACTURED FOOD IN THE PHLOEM
sections.prompt: The maize plant's leaves manufacture glucose through photosynthesis during the sunny Kericho afternoon. This sugar must be transported to the roots, the developing maize cob, and the growing stem tip. Explain how this transport occurs. In your answer: (a) name the tissue responsible for transporting manufactured food substances and describe its structure, distinguishing it from xylem; (b) explain what 'translocation' means and name the main form in which sugar is transported; (c) describe the pressure-flow (mass flow) hypothesis for how sugars move through this tissue, making clear the roles of 'source' and 'sink'; (d) explain why translocation, unlike the transpiration stream, can move substances in two directions simultaneously in the plant; and (e) connect the process of translocation to the farmer's interest in a high-yielding maize cob, explaining what determines how much sugar reaches the developing grain.
sections.exemplar:
The transport of manufactured food substances — primarily sucrose — from the leaves to the rest of the maize plant occurs through the phloem tissue, in a process called translocation. Phloem differs from xylem in several important ways: phloem is composed of living sieve tube elements connected end-to-end with perforated sieve plates, accompanied by companion cells that provide the metabolic energy for loading and unloading sugars. Unlike xylem, which carries water upward only, phloem can transport sucrose and amino acids in any direction — upward to growing shoot tips or downward to roots and developing maize cobs.

Translocation is explained by the pressure-flow (mass flow) hypothesis. At a 'source' — any organ that produces or releases sucrose, primarily the photosynthesising leaves — companion cells use active transport (requiring ATP) to load sucrose into the phloem sieve tubes. This lowers the water potential inside the sieve tubes at the source, so water enters from adjacent xylem by osmosis, creating a high hydrostatic pressure. At a 'sink' — any organ that consumes or stores sucrose, such as the developing maize cob, the roots, or the growing stem tip — sucrose is continually removed by active transport into surrounding cells for respiration or conversion to starch. This raises the water potential in the phloem at the sink, water leaves by osmosis into the surrounding tissue, and the hydrostatic pressure drops. The resulting pressure gradient from source (high pressure) to sink (low pressure) drives a mass flow of sucrose solution along the sieve tubes. Because different sieve tubes can carry sucrose in different directions at the same time — some going down to the roots while others go up to the shoot tip — translocation is bidirectional, unlike the strictly upward transpiration stream in the xylem.

For the farmer in Kericho, the size and sweetness of her maize cob depend directly on how effectively sucrose produced in the leaves is translocated to the cob as a sink. Plants with more leaves, more chlorophyll (ensured by adequate nitrogen fertiliser), and healthy phloem will produce a stronger pressure gradient and channel more sucrose to the grain, resulting in a higher-yielding, better-filled cob. This is why practices such as removing damaged leaves or applying fertiliser at the right time directly affect the farmer's harvest.

rubric.criterion: Scientific Accuracy of Processes and Structures
rubric.excellent: All biological processes (osmosis, transpiration, cohesion-tension, active transport, translocation, mass flow) are explained correctly and completely. Structures (xylem, phloem, guard cells, root hair cells, Casparian strip, sieve tubes, companion cells) are named and described accurately. No significant factual errors are present.
rubric.proficient: Most biological processes and structures are named and explained correctly. Minor inaccuracies or omissions are present but do not undermine the overall explanation. The student demonstrates a solid conceptual understanding of transport in plants.
rubric.developing: Several key processes or structures are misidentified, confused, or missing. Explanations contain significant factual errors (e.g., confusing xylem and phloem functions, stating that osmosis requires energy, or incorrectly describing the direction of translocation). Understanding is partial or fragmented.

rubric.excellent: Scientific terminology (osmosis, water potential, transpiration, cohesion, adhesion, translocation, turgor pressure, plasmolysis, source, sink, apoplast, symplast, root pressure, Casparian strip) is used consistently, correctly, and in context throughout all sections. Technical language enhances clarity rather than obscuring meaning.
rubric.proficient: Most key scientific terms are used correctly. Occasional terms are missing or used imprecisely, but the student's meaning is generally clear. At least 8–10 of the key vocabulary terms from the unit are used appropriately.
rubric.developing: Few scientific terms are used, and those that appear are often used incorrectly or interchangeably (e.g., 'diffusion' used where 'osmosis' is needed, or 'veins' used instead of 'xylem vessels'). The student relies mainly on everyday language, which limits the precision of the explanation.

rubric.criterion: Connection to the Kericho Wilting Phenomenon
rubric.excellent: The student explicitly and repeatedly connects their scientific explanations back to the phenomenon of the wilting Kericho maize plants. The afternoon wilting and evening recovery are fully explained using cohesion-tension, stomatal behaviour, transpiration rate vs. uptake rate, and the role of guard cells. The student's explanation resolves the puzzle posed at the start of the unit in a coherent, complete narrative.
rubric.proficient: The student connects most explanations to the Kericho phenomenon and provides a reasonable account of why the plants wilt and recover. Some sections address the phenomenon only briefly or indirectly. The overall narrative is mostly coherent but one or two key links (e.g., the role of evening conditions in closing stomata) may be underdeveloped.
rubric.developing: The student makes little or no explicit connection to the wilting phenomenon. Explanations are generic and could apply to any plant context. The student does not clearly explain why the Kericho maize wilts in the afternoon or recovers in the evening, leaving the central puzzle unresolved.

rubric.criterion: Depth of Explanation and Use of Evidence
rubric.excellent: Explanations go beyond naming processes to describe underlying mechanisms (e.g., hydrogen bonding for cohesion, potassium ion pumping for stomatal opening, hydrostatic pressure gradient for mass flow). The student references evidence gathered during investigations (e.g., the effect of light on stomatal aperture, the effect of wind on transpiration rate) to support claims. Answers in all five sections are thorough and detailed.
rubric.proficient: Explanations describe mechanisms at a satisfactory level of depth. Most answers include a 'how' and 'why' rather than just naming processes. Some evidence from class investigations is referenced. One or two sections may be less developed than others but still demonstrate understanding above a surface level.
rubric.developing: Explanations are mostly surface-level, listing facts or naming processes without explaining how or why they occur. Little or no reference is made to evidence from investigations. Answers are brief and lack the mechanistic detail needed to fully explain transport in plants.

rubric.excellent: The written response is well-organised, with a logical flow within each section and clear paragraphing. Scientific ideas are sequenced in a way that builds a coherent, complete explanation. The diagram of the stoma (Section 3) is clearly drawn, fully annotated with all required labels (guard cells, stoma, chloroplast, nucleus, thick inner wall, thin outer wall — open and closed states), and directly supports the written explanation.
rubric.proficient: The response is generally well-organised and ideas are presented in a logical order. The diagram is present and mostly correctly labelled, though one or two labels may be missing or inaccurately placed. The writing is clear enough that the reader can follow the student's reasoning without difficulty.
rubric.developing: The response lacks clear organisation; ideas are presented in a random or repetitive order that makes the explanation hard to follow. The diagram is missing, unlabelled, or contains significant errors that show confusion about stomatal structure. The student's reasoning is difficult to follow in multiple sections.

SUMMARY_TABLE.drivingQuestion: DRIVING QUESTION: How does water get from the soil all the way to the top leaves of a tall maize plant — and why does the plant sometimes lose water faster than it can take it up?

lessons.title: Lesson 1 — Anchoring Phenomenon: The Wilting Maize Plant
lessons.observed: Learners examined photographs and a short scenario of a maize plant in a Rift Valley farm that wilted on a hot, dry afternoon despite being watered the previous evening. They recorded initial observations about where water enters the plant, how it might travel upward, and why the plant might lose water faster than it gains it.
lessons.learned: Learners activated prior knowledge about plant structures (roots, stem, leaves) and generated their first set of questions for the Driving Question Board. They recognised that something inside the plant must move water upward against gravity and that leaves must play a role in water loss.
lessons.explained: The wilting maize plant cannot be fully explained yet — it is the mystery that anchors the entire sub-strand. Learners' initial models showed water entering roots and somehow reaching leaves, but could not yet account for the driving force or the mechanism of water loss.

lessons.title: Lesson 2 — Structure of Transport Tissues: Xylem and Phloem
lessons.observed: Learners examined prepared slides and detailed diagrams of cross-sections of a maize stem and root, identifying xylem vessels (dead, hollow, lignified, no end walls) and phloem sieve tubes with companion cells. They annotated diagrams and compared the two tissue types in a structured table.
lessons.learned: Xylem vessels are dead, hollow, lignified tubes without end walls — a structure perfectly suited for rapid, unobstructed upward flow of water and mineral salts. Phloem sieve tubes are living, have sieve plates, and are associated with companion cells that provide metabolic support for active translocation of sugars.
lessons.explained: The tall maize plant has a continuous pipeline of xylem vessels running from root to leaf tip. The structural features of xylem (no end walls, lignified walls, hollow lumen) explain how an unbroken water column can be maintained. Phloem's living nature explains why it can transport sugars in multiple directions, unlike xylem.

lessons.title: Lesson 3 — Water and Mineral Salt Uptake: From Soil to Xylem
lessons.observed: Learners carried out an osmosis investigation using beetroot cylinders placed in solutions of different concentrations, simulating the water potential gradient between soil water and root hair cell sap. They also studied annotated diagrams of root hair cells and traced the apoplast and symplast pathways across the root cortex to the xylem.
lessons.learned: Water enters root hair cells by osmosis, moving from high water potential in the soil solution to lower water potential in the root hair cell sap. Mineral salts are absorbed by active transport, requiring ATP from companion cells. Water moves across the root cortex via the apoplast (cell walls) and symplast (cytoplasm) pathways before entering xylem.
lessons.explained: For the maize plant, the first step of the journey is explained: water in the soil around the roots enters root hair cells passively by osmosis. The Casparian strip forces water through the symplast at the endodermis, allowing the plant to control what enters the xylem. Active uptake of mineral salts lowers the water potential inside xylem, helping draw more water in.

lessons.title: Lesson 4 — Transpiration: Process and Significance
lessons.observed: Learners observed a demonstration of a potted plant sealed in a clear plastic bag placed in sunlight near a window in the classroom, noting water droplets accumulating on the inside of the bag. They also examined diagrams of an open stoma and the surrounding spongy mesophyll air spaces, tracing the path of water vapour from mesophyll cell surfaces to the atmosphere.
lessons.learned: Transpiration is the loss of water vapour from aerial parts of a plant, primarily through stomata. Water evaporates from the wet surfaces of mesophyll cells into the sub-stomatal air spaces, then diffuses out through open stomata down a concentration gradient. This evaporation lowers the water potential of mesophyll cells, creating a pull — the transpiration pull — that draws water up the xylem by cohesion-tension.
lessons.explained: The maize plant loses water through its leaves via transpiration. On a hot, dry, windy afternoon in the Rift Valley, stomata open in response to light and the concentration gradient between humid air inside the leaf and dry air outside becomes very steep — causing rapid water loss. This begins to explain why the maize plant can lose water faster than its roots can absorb it.

lessons.title: Lesson 5 — Factors Affecting Transpiration: Potometer Practical Investigation
lessons.observed: Learners used a simple bubble potometer (constructed from a drinking straw, rubber tubing, and a ruler) with a leafy maize or Tradescantia shoot to measure the rate of water uptake as a proxy for transpiration rate. They systematically changed one variable at a time — fanning the shoot (wind), shining a torch on it (light intensity), and wrapping it in a plastic bag (humidity) — and recorded bubble movement over fixed time intervals.
lessons.learned: Transpiration rate increases with: higher light intensity (stomata open wider), higher temperature (faster evaporation and diffusion), increased wind speed (removes water vapour from leaf surface, steepening the diffusion gradient), and lower humidity (increases the water vapour concentration gradient). High humidity and darkness reduce transpiration rate.
lessons.explained: The potometer data give learners direct evidence for why the Rift Valley maize plant wilts on a hot, bright, windy afternoon. All three conditions — high light, high temperature, and wind — act together to maximise transpiration rate, causing water loss to outstrip uptake. Learners updated their DQB and initial models with these factors.

lessons.title: Lesson 6 — Analysing Transpiration Data and Plant Adaptations to Water Loss
lessons.observed: Learners analysed a provided data set showing transpiration rates of maize plants measured at different times of day in Kericho and compared them with data from a xerophyte (cactus). They also examined photographs and diagrams of xerophyte adaptations: sunken stomata, thick cuticle, reduced leaf surface area, and CAM photosynthesis timing.
lessons.learned: Environmental factors — especially light intensity and wind — accelerate transpiration by causing stomata to open wider or by increasing the diffusion gradient for water vapour. Plants adapted to arid environments (xerophytes) possess structural and behavioural adaptations that reduce transpiration without completely halting gas exchange needed for photosynthesis. Maize lacks extreme xerophyte adaptations, making it vulnerable to water stress.
lessons.explained: The data analysis confirms the pattern seen in the potometer practical. Learners can now explain why a maize plant in the Rift Valley is particularly vulnerable during the dry season: it has relatively large leaves, a thin cuticle, and abundant stomata compared with xerophytes. Farmers in arid areas of Kenya who grow drought-tolerant varieties are applying the same principles of reducing water loss.

lessons.title: Lesson 7 — Translocation in Phloem: How Sugars Travel from Leaves to Roots and Fruits
lessons.observed: Learners studied the results of a classic ringing (girdling) experiment — illustrated with a diagram of a tree branch where phloem was removed in a ring, causing sugar to accumulate above the ring as a swelling — and related this to the role of phloem. They also examined an animated diagram of the mass flow hypothesis, tracing sucrose loading at source leaves and unloading at sink organs such as developing maize cobs and roots.
lessons.learned: Translocation is the movement of sucrose and amino acids through phloem sieve tubes from sources (photosynthesising leaves) to sinks (roots, storage organs, and developing fruits such as the maize cob). The mass flow hypothesis proposes that active loading of sucrose into phloem at the source lowers water potential, drawing water in by osmosis, creating high pressure that pushes sap toward sinks where sucrose is unloaded.
lessons.explained: While xylem explains water movement, phloem explains how the products of photosynthesis reach the developing maize cob and roots. Learners can now distinguish the two transport systems: xylem carries water and minerals upward (driven by transpiration pull), while phloem carries sugars in any direction needed (driven by pressure flow). Both are essential for the maize plant's growth and survival.

lessons.title: Lesson 8 — Model Revision, Synthesis and Final Explanation: Solving the Mystery of the Wilting Maize Plant
lessons.observed: Learners reviewed all evidence gathered across lessons 1–7: osmosis data, root hair diagrams, potometer results, transpiration data tables, phloem ringing diagrams, and all DQB entries. They worked in groups to produce a final annotated model of transport in the maize plant, tracing water from soil → root hair → cortex → xylem → stem → leaf mesophyll → stomata → atmosphere, and sucrose from leaf → phloem → maize cob.
lessons.learned: Learners synthesised that: (1) water enters root hair cells by osmosis down a water potential gradient; (2) cohesion-tension driven by transpiration pulls a continuous water column up xylem; (3) transpiration rate is governed by light, temperature, wind, and humidity; (4) when environmental conditions maximise transpiration faster than roots can absorb water, wilting occurs; and (5) phloem translocates sugars by mass flow from source to sink, independent of xylem flow direction.
lessons.explained: The driving question is fully answered: water reaches the top leaves of the tall maize plant because transpiration from leaves creates a tension that pulls a cohesive water column up through continuous xylem vessels, supplied by osmotic uptake at root hairs. The maize plant wilts when hot, dry, windy conditions in the Rift Valley cause transpiration to outpace absorption — a water deficit that closes stomata as a last resort, also slowing photosynthesis and growth of the valuable maize cob.
